points: Each pixel gets a class + instance ID

sections.heading: Segmentation Evaluation Metrics
sections.text:
**Intersection over Union (IoU / Jaccard)** for class $c$:
$$\mathrm{IoU}_c = \frac{|P_c \cap G_c|}{|P_c \cup G_c|}$$

**Mean IoU (mIoU)**: average IoU across all classes.

**Dice Coefficient (F1 for pixel sets)**:
$$\mathrm{Dice} = \frac{2|P \cap G|}{|P| + |G|}$$

For instance segmentation: **AP** (Average Precision) at IoU thresholds 0.5–0.95 (COCO metric).

ida05.title: U-Net: Encoder-Decoder for Biomedical Segmentation
ida05.summary: U-Net (Ronneberger et al. 2015) introduced the encoder-decoder with skip connections for biomedical image segmentation. The skip connections preserve fine spatial detail lost during downsampling, achieving state-of-the-art results with very limited training data.

sections.heading: U-Net Architecture: Encoder, Bottleneck, Decoder
sections.text: The encoder (contracting path) applies repeated 3×3 conv + ReLU + max-pool blocks, halving spatial resolution at each step while doubling channels: 64→128→256→512→1024. This builds a hierarchical feature representation. The decoder (expansive path) mirrors the encoder: transposed convolutions upsample the feature map, and skip connections concatenate the corresponding encoder feature map (same resolution). The concatenated features are then processed by 3×3 convolutions. Finally, a 1×1 convolution produces the class probability map. The skip connections are the key innovation: they pass fine-grained spatial information (exact cell boundaries, texture) directly to the decoder, bypassing the lossy bottleneck.

sections.heading: Why U-Net Excels with Limited Data
sections.text: The original U-Net paper achieved state-of-the-art results on the ISBI cell segmentation challenge using only 30 training images. Two factors: (1) The architecture itself encodes strong inductive biases for segmentation — locality, multi-scale context, skip connections. (2) Extensive data augmentation, especially elastic deformations that simulate the deformable nature of biological tissue. The weighted cross-entropy loss additionally up-weights pixels near cell borders, helping the network separate touching cells. Modern variants (ResUNet, Attention U-Net, TransUNet) further improve performance but the core encoder-decoder-skip connection design remains standard.

sections.heading: Weighted Loss for Cell Border Separation
sections.text:
U-Net uses a weighted binary cross-entropy to emphasise boundaries between touching cells:

$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{x} w(x) \cdot \mathcal{L}_{CE}(\hat{p}(x), y(x))$$

where $w(x) = w_c(x) + w_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{(d_1(x)+d_2(x))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$

$d_1(x)$: distance from $x$ to nearest cell, $d_2(x)$: distance to second nearest. The exponential peaks in the narrow gap between touching cells, encouraging the network to predict background (border) between them.

ida05.title: Mask R-CNN and Instance Segmentation
ida05.summary: Mask R-CNN extends Faster R-CNN by adding a small FCN mask head parallel to the box/class head. Combined with RoI Align (replacing RoI Pooling), it predicts per-instance binary masks without interfering with classification performance.
ida05.context: Lecture 05 · 16.11.2023 — Instance segmentation with Mask R-CNN

sections.heading: The Mask Head: Parallel to Detection
sections.text: Mask R-CNN adds a third branch to Faster R-CNN alongside the class and box regression heads. After RoI Align produces a fixed-size feature for each region proposal, the mask head applies a series of 3×3 convolutions followed by a transposed convolution to upsample to a 28×28 binary mask per class. Crucially, the mask head predicts a mask for EACH class independently — there is no competition between classes. The final binary mask for a detected object is the mask of its predicted class. This decoupling prevents the mask quality from being affected by classification difficulty.

left.title: RoI Pooling
points: Quantisation error: up to 1 pixel misalignment

right.title: RoI Align
points: Mask AP improves ~3% vs RoI Pooling

ida05.title: Cellpose and StarDist: Biology-Inspired Segmentation
ida05.summary: Cellpose and StarDist are purpose-built instance segmentation methods for biological cells. Cellpose predicts vector flows toward each cell centre; StarDist represents cells as star-convex polygons. Both dramatically outperform generic methods on biological imagery.

sections.heading: Cellpose: Flow-Based Cell Segmentation
sections.text: Cellpose (Stringer et al. 2021) is motivated by the observation that cells come in highly variable shapes, but they all have a centre. The network predicts two 'flow fields': horizontal and vertical gradients pointing toward each cell's centre (like a vector field pointing inward). A pixel at the boundary of a cell receives a flow vector pointing toward that cell's centre. After prediction, simulated particles are placed at each pixel and follow the flow field — particles belonging to the same cell converge to the same centre. Clustering these trajectories gives instance segmentation. This representation is much more robust to shape variability than explicit mask prediction.

sections.heading: StarDist: Star-Convex Polygon Representation
sections.text: StarDist (Schmidt et al. 2018) represents each cell as a star-convex polygon — a shape where every point on the boundary is visible from the centre. The network predicts, for each pixel: (1) the probability that it is a cell centre, and (2) $n$ radial distances $r_1, \ldots, r_n$ in $n$ equally-spaced directions. These $n$ radial distances define the polygon boundary from the predicted centre. Non-maximum suppression selects the best polygon proposals. Star-convex polygons work extremely well for roundish or elliptical cells (most common in 2D histology and microscopy) but fail for highly elongated or branching shapes where star-convexity doesn't hold.

ida06.title: Kalman Filter for Object Tracking
ida06.summary: The Kalman filter is the optimal linear estimator for tracking objects under Gaussian noise. It alternates between a predict step (propagate state estimate forward using a motion model) and an update step (correct with new observations). Essential for multi-object tracking (MOT).

sections.heading: Kalman Filter: Predict and Update
sections.text:
State vector $\mathbf{x}_t = [x, y, v_x, v_y]^\top$ (position + velocity).

**Predict step** (time update):
$$\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t|t-1} = F\, \mathbf{x}_{t-1|t-1}$$
$$P_{t|t-1} = F\, P_{t-1|t-1}\, F^\top + Q$$

**Update step** (measurement update) given observation $\mathbf{z}_t$:
$$K_t = P_{t|t-1} H^\top (H P_{t|t-1} H^\top + R)^{-1} \quad \text{(Kalman gain)}$$
$$\mathbf{x}_{t|t} = \hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t|t-1} + K_t(\mathbf{z}_t - H\hat{\mathbf{x}}_{t|t-1})$$
$$P_{t|t} = (I - K_t H)\, P_{t|t-1}$$

$F$: state transition, $H$: observation model, $Q$: process noise, $R$: measurement noise.

sections.heading: Intuition: Predict then Correct
sections.text: Prediction: using the motion model (e.g. constant velocity), predict where the object will be at time $t$ without any new measurement. This gives a Gaussian estimate with growing uncertainty ($P$ increases by $Q$ at each step). Update: when a detection arrives, the Kalman gain $K_t$ decides how much to trust the prediction vs the measurement. If measurement noise $R$ is small (accurate detector), $K_t \approx 1$ and the estimate snaps to the measurement. If $R$ is large (noisy detector), $K_t \approx 0$ and the prediction dominates. The Kalman filter is optimal (minimum variance unbiased estimator) under the Gaussian linear assumptions.

left.title: Kalman Filter
points: Optimal for linear systems + Gaussian noise

right.title: Particle Filter
points: Represents distribution as N weighted particles
Expensive: O(N) per step, N ~1000–10000 particles

ida06.title: Hungarian Algorithm and Multi-Object Tracking
ida06.summary: Multi-object tracking (MOT) is a two-step process: detect objects in each frame, then associate detections across frames. The Hungarian algorithm solves the bipartite matching assignment problem optimally — matching predicted track positions to new detections.

sections.heading: The Tracking-by-Detection Paradigm
sections.text: Modern multi-object tracking follows the tracking-by-detection paradigm: (1) run a detector on each frame independently to get a set of bounding boxes; (2) associate detections across frames using the Hungarian algorithm. The association cost between track $i$ and detection $j$ is typically the IoU between the predicted box (from Kalman filter) and the detected box — lower IoU = higher cost. The Hungarian algorithm finds the globally optimal assignment (minimising total cost) in $O(n^3)$ time. Unmatched tracks are marked as 'lost' (and deleted after $T$ frames); unmatched detections initialise new tracks. This is the core of SORT (Simple Online and Realtime Tracking).

sections.heading: Assignment Cost Matrix
sections.text:
With $M$ tracks and $N$ detections, cost matrix $C \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times N}$:

$$C_{ij} = 1 - \mathrm{IoU}(\hat{b}_i, d_j)$$

where $\hat{b}_i$ is the Kalman-predicted box for track $i$, $d_j$ is detection $j$.

Hungarian algorithm finds assignment $\sigma$ minimising $\sum_i C_{i,\sigma(i)}$.

**DeepSORT** extends SORT by also using appearance features (CNN embedding of the detection crop) as an additional cost:
$$C_{ij} = \lambda \cdot (1-\mathrm{IoU}) + (1-\lambda) \cdot d_{\cos}(f_i, f_j)$$

ida06.title: Optical Flow and Deep Learning Trackers
ida06.summary: Optical flow estimates per-pixel motion between frames. FlowNet introduced end-to-end deep optical flow estimation. GOTURN was among the first deep learning object trackers, framing tracking as regression from a siamese CNN.
ida06.context: Lecture 06 · 23.11.2023 — Deep approaches to motion estimation

sections.heading: Optical Flow Constraint Equation
sections.text:
Assuming brightness constancy: pixel intensity does not change between frames:
$$I(x, y, t) = I(x+u, y+v, t+1)$$

Taylor expansion gives the **Optical Flow Constraint**:
$$I_x \cdot u + I_y \cdot v + I_t = 0$$

where $I_x, I_y$ are spatial gradients and $I_t$ is the temporal gradient. This is one equation with two unknowns $(u,v)$ — the **aperture problem**. Additional constraints needed: smoothness (Horn-Schunck: minimise $|\nabla u|^2 + |\nabla v|^2$) or local neighbourhood (Lucas-Kanade: assume constant flow in a patch).

sections.heading: FlowNet: End-to-End Deep Optical Flow
sections.text: FlowNet (Dosovitskiy et al. 2015) concatenated two consecutive frames as a 6-channel input to a CNN encoder-decoder (with skip connections like U-Net). FlowNetC added a correlation layer: compute the dot product between feature maps at different spatial offsets, producing an explicit cost volume of matching costs for each displacement. The network learns to produce a dense flow field ($u,v$ per pixel). Training required a synthetic dataset (FlyingChairs — CGI chairs flying over random backgrounds) because ground-truth optical flow is impossible to measure in real scenes. FlowNet2 stacked multiple FlowNets for refinement. RAFT (2020) replaced the architecture with iterative updates on a 4D cost volume, dramatically improving accuracy.

ida07.title: From RNNs to Self-Attention in Vision
ida07.summary: RNNs modelled sequential data but struggled with long-range dependencies and couldn't parallelise. Self-attention allows every pixel/patch to directly attend to every other, enabling global context modelling in a single layer. This is the key motivation for applying Transformers to images.

sections.heading: Why Not Use RNNs for Images?
sections.text: Images processed as sequences (raster scan) impose an artificial 1D ordering on 2D data. RNNs then struggle with both local 2D spatial relationships (nearby pixels in 2D may be far apart in the sequence) and long-range dependencies (gradients vanish over 100s of steps). CNNs solve locality via local receptive fields, but building a global receptive field requires many layers. Self-attention has $O(1)$ path length between any two positions — every patch can directly access context from any other patch in the image in a single layer, regardless of distance. The cost is $O(n^2)$ in the number of patches, which is why image patches (not individual pixels) are the standard unit of computation.

sections.heading: Self-Attention for Visual Features
sections.text:
Given feature map $X \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times d}$ (N positions, d-dim features):

$$Q = XW_Q,\quad K = XW_K,\quad V = XW_V$$

$$\mathrm{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(\frac{QK^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V$$

Attention weight $\alpha_{ij}$ = how much position $i$ attends to position $j$.

For images: $N = H/P \times W/P$ patches of size $P\times P$. ViT uses $P=16$, so a $224\times224$ image becomes $14\times14=196$ tokens.

ida07.title: Vision Transformer (ViT)
ida07.summary: ViT (Dosovitskiy et al. 2020) applies the standard NLP Transformer directly to sequences of image patches. With sufficient data and scale, it matches or surpasses CNN performance, showing that the inductive biases of CNNs (locality, equivariance) can be learned rather than hard-coded.

sections.heading: ViT Patch Embedding and Class Token
sections.text:
Image $x \in \mathbb{R}^{H\times W\times C}$ split into $N$ patches of size $P\times P$:
$$x_p \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times(P^2C)},\quad N = HW/P^2$$

Linear projection to $d$-dim embeddings:
$$z_0 = [x_{\mathrm{cls}};\; x_p^1 E;\; x_p^2 E;\; \ldots;\; x_p^N E] + E_{\mathrm{pos}}$$

$x_{\mathrm{cls}}$: learnable classification token. $E_{\mathrm{pos}}$: positional embedding (learnable 1D positions).

Standard Transformer encoder $L$ times → $z_L$. Classification: $y = \mathrm{MLP}(z_L^0)$ (class token output).

sections.heading: Data Hunger and Pretraining
sections.text: The fundamental limitation of ViT is data efficiency: CNNs encode translation equivariance and locality as inductive biases, enabling learning from tens of thousands of images. ViT must learn these from scratch, requiring millions of images (JFT-300M, ImageNet-21k). On ImageNet-1k alone, ViT underperforms ResNet. This motivated DeiT (Data-efficient Image Transformers), which uses knowledge distillation from a CNN teacher + heavy augmentation to match ViT/JFT-300M performance with only ImageNet-1k. Later work (MAE — Masked Autoencoders) showed that masking 75% of patches and predicting the masked content is an extremely effective self-supervised pretraining strategy, making ViT competitive even with limited labeled data.

left.title: CNN
points: Global context only after many layers

right.title: ViT
points: Scales better: performance ∝ data and params

ida07.title: DETR: Detection with Transformers
ida07.summary: DETR (Carion et al. 2020) reformulates object detection as a set prediction problem. It eliminates hand-crafted anchors and NMS entirely, using learned object queries and bipartite matching to produce exactly N detections per image.

sections.heading: Set Prediction with Bipartite Matching
sections.text: DETR predicts a fixed set of $N=100$ object hypotheses (most will be 'no object'). Training requires matching predictions to ground truth annotations — but unlike standard detectors with anchors, there's no natural correspondence. DETR uses the Hungarian algorithm to find the optimal bipartite matching $\hat{\sigma}$ minimising the total matching cost: $\hat{\sigma} = \arg\min_{\sigma} \sum_i [\mathcal{L}_{cls}(p_i, c_{\sigma(i)}) + \mathcal{L}_{box}(b_i, b_{\sigma(i)})]$. Each ground-truth object is matched to exactly one prediction. The matched pairs then compute standard cross-entropy (class) and L1+GIoU (box) losses. Unmatched predictions are assigned the 'no object' class.

sections.heading: Object Queries and Cross-Attention
sections.text:
DETR architecture:
1. CNN backbone → feature map $\in \mathbb{R}^{H'\times W' \times C}$
2. Flatten + Transformer encoder (self-attention over spatial positions)
3. $N$ learnable **object queries** $q \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times d}$ attend to encoder output via cross-attention
4. Each query output → class prediction + bounding box ($x_c, y_c, w, h$)

The object queries specialise during training: some queries learn to detect large objects, others small, others at specific locations. They are not anchors — they have no pre-defined size or position.

ida07.title: Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Window Attention
ida07.summary: Swin Transformer (Liu et al. 2021) restores the hierarchical multi-scale feature maps of CNNs while using self-attention. It computes attention within local windows (not globally), reducing complexity from O(N²) to O(N), and shifts windows between layers for cross-window communication.

sections.heading: Window Attention and Shifted Windows
sections.text: Global self-attention on a high-resolution image is computationally prohibitive: a 224×224 image with 4×4 patches has $56^2 = 3136$ tokens, giving $3136^2 \approx 10M$ attention pairs per head. Swin partitions the feature map into non-overlapping windows of size $M\times M$ (e.g. $M=7$) and computes self-attention within each window independently. Complexity is now $O(M^2 N)$ — linear in the number of tokens $N$. But windows don't communicate! The 'shifted window' scheme (SW-MSA) shifts the partition by $(M/2, M/2)$ in alternating layers, ensuring that tokens near window boundaries get to attend to tokens across the boundary in the next layer.

sections.heading: Hierarchical Feature Maps for Dense Prediction
sections.text: Unlike ViT (single resolution output), Swin produces multi-scale feature maps: Stage 1 (56×56), Stage 2 (28×28), Stage 3 (14×14), Stage 4 (7×7) by patch merging (concatenating 2×2 neighbouring patches and applying a linear layer to halve the spatial dimension while doubling channels). This makes Swin a drop-in backbone for FPN-based detection (Swin + FPN + Cascade R-CNN achieves 58.7 box AP on COCO) and U-Net-style segmentation (Swin-UNet), tasks that require multi-scale features. Swin has become the standard backbone for many state-of-the-art image analysis systems.

ida08.title: UNETR: Transformers for Medical Image Segmentation
ida08.summary: UNETR (Hatamizadeh et al. 2022) uses a ViT encoder to extract global context from volumetric medical images, connected to a CNN decoder via skip connections in a U-shaped architecture. It bridges the global representation strength of Transformers with the spatial precision of U-Net.

sections.heading: Architecture: ViT Encoder + CNN Decoder
sections.text: UNETR splits a 3D medical volume (CT or MRI) into 3D patches (16×16×16) and processes them with a standard ViT encoder. The key insight: intermediate representations from different Transformer layers are extracted and connected as skip connections to the CNN decoder. Early ViT layers capture local texture; later layers capture global anatomy. The decoder uses 3D transposed convolutions to upsample and progressively refine the segmentation. This U-Net-like design means UNETR benefits from both the global context modelling of Transformers and the precise spatial localisation of CNNs. UNETR achieves state-of-the-art on the Medical Segmentation Decathlon benchmark.

sections.heading: U-Net vs UNETR for Medical Segmentation

right.title: UNETR
points: ViT encoder: global attention from layer 1
Better for tasks needing global reasoning

ida08.title: SAM: Segment Anything Model
ida08.summary: SAM (Kirillov et al. 2023) is a foundation model for image segmentation. Trained on 1 billion masks, it accepts flexible prompts (points, boxes, text, free-form) and produces high-quality masks for any object in any image — without task-specific fine-tuning.

sections.heading: SAM Architecture: Image Encoder + Prompt Encoder + Mask Decoder
sections.text: SAM has three components. (1) Image encoder: a MAE-pretrained ViT-H processes the image once and produces dense image embeddings. This is the expensive part (done offline or cached). (2) Prompt encoder: handles different prompt types — sparse prompts (points, boxes) use positional encoding + learned tokens; dense prompts (masks) use convolutions. (3) Mask decoder: a lightweight Transformer decoder that attends from prompt tokens to image embeddings, predicting 3 mask candidates (and their quality scores) in real-time (<50ms). The three-mask output handles ambiguity: if a point clicks on a dog, the model offers masks for the dog's leg, body, and entire dog.

sections.heading: The SA-1B Dataset and Promptable Segmentation
sections.text: SAM was trained on SA-1B: 11 million images, 1 billion masks collected semi-automatically (human annotators prompted SAM iteratively). The scale of training data is unprecedented for segmentation. SAM enables zero-shot transfer: without any fine-tuning, it segments objects in medical images, aerial imagery, microscopy, and artistic paintings — domains completely absent from training. The promptable interface is key: rather than training separate models for each task, a single model with a flexible prompt interface can solve any segmentation task interactively. SAM2 extends this to video with a streaming memory mechanism.

sections.heading: SAM Prompt Types and Use Cases
items: Point prompt: click on foreground (positive) or background (negative) points
Box prompt: draw a bounding box around the region of interest
Mask prompt: provide a coarse mask to refine
Automatic mode: grid of points → generates all masks → filtered by quality score
Application: interactive annotation tools for training data collection
Limitation: struggles with very thin structures and high ambiguity scenarios

ida08.title: Self-Supervised Pretraining: MAE and DINO
ida08.summary: Masked Autoencoders (MAE) and DINO showed that ViTs can be pretrained self-supervisedly on unlabeled images, learning rich visual representations without any labels — enabling transfer to downstream tasks with minimal fine-tuning.

sections.heading: MAE: Masked Autoencoder for Vision
sections.text: MAE (He et al. 2022) randomly masks 75% of image patches and trains a ViT encoder-decoder to reconstruct the masked patches in pixel space. The key: only the visible (unmasked) tokens are fed to the encoder — making it highly efficient. The decoder (lightweight, 8 layers vs encoder's 24) reconstructs missing patches from the encoder's output + learnable mask tokens. The high masking ratio forces the model to learn holistic image understanding rather than simple interpolation from adjacent patches. MAE pretraining on ImageNet-1k produces features that transfer excellently to classification (linear probe), detection, and segmentation — matching or beating supervised baselines.

sections.heading: DINO: Self-Distillation with No Labels
sections.text: DINO (Caron et al. 2021) uses a student-teacher self-distillation framework: two ViTs (student and teacher), the teacher being an exponential moving average (EMA) of the student. Both receive different augmented views of the same image; the student is trained to match the teacher's class token output (via cross-entropy with a sharpened softmax). DINO reveals remarkable emergent properties: the attention maps in the final layer spontaneously segment the main object from the background without any segmentation supervision. DINO features work well for nearest-neighbor classification without any fine-tuning, demonstrating genuinely semantic representations.

ida09.title: Point Cloud Representation and Preprocessing
ida09.summary: A point cloud is an unordered set of 3D points sampled from a surface. Unlike voxels (regular 3D grid) or meshes (connectivity structure), point clouds are sparse and orderless. Preprocessing — normal estimation, outlier removal, downsampling — is critical before any learning.

sections.heading: Point Cloud Definition
sections.text:
Point cloud: $\mathcal{P} = \{\mathbf{p}_1, \mathbf{p}_2, \ldots, \mathbf{p}_N\}$ where $\mathbf{p}_i \in \mathbb{R}^D$.

Typically $D=3$ (XYZ coordinates); may include additional features: RGB colour, intensity, normals.

**Key properties**:
- Unordered: no canonical ordering of points
- Irregular: non-uniform density (near objects → dense, far → sparse)
- Variable size: $N$ varies per scene or object

Neighbourhood queries:
- $k$-NN: $k$ nearest neighbours by Euclidean distance
- Radius search: all points within radius $r$: $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{p}) = \{\mathbf{q} : \|\mathbf{p}-\mathbf{q}\|_2 < r\}$

sections.heading: Surface Normal Estimation
sections.text: Surface normals are essential for shape descriptors, rendering, and many downstream tasks. Estimation via PCA on local neighbourhoods: for point $\mathbf{p}$ with $k$ nearest neighbours $\{\mathbf{q}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{q}_k\}$, compute the covariance matrix $C = \frac{1}{k}\sum_i (\mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}})(\mathbf{q}_i - \bar{\mathbf{q}})^\top$ and take the eigenvector corresponding to the smallest eigenvalue as the normal direction. Normal orientation (inward/outward) is ambiguous and requires a reference (e.g. consistently orient toward the sensor). Normal quality depends on neighbourhood size: too small → noisy; too large → over-smooth, fails at sharp edges.

sections.heading: Standard Preprocessing Steps
items: Voxel downsampling: replace all points in each voxel with their centroid — reduces density to uniform
Statistical outlier removal: remove points whose mean distance to k-NN is more than N·std above the mean
Radius outlier removal: remove points with fewer than M neighbours within radius r
Normal estimation via PCA on k-NN neighbourhoods
Point cloud registration (ICP): align two overlapping point clouds into a common coordinate frame
Normalisation: translate centroid to origin, scale to unit sphere for network inputs

ida09.title: PointNet: Permutation-Invariant Learning
ida09.summary: PointNet (Qi et al. 2017) was the first deep learning architecture to directly consume unordered point clouds. It achieves permutation invariance via a symmetric function (global max pooling) and handles rigid transformations via a learnable T-Net alignment module.

sections.heading: PointNet: Symmetric Function Formulation
sections.text:
A function $f$ on a point set is permutation invariant if:
$$f(\mathbf{p}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{p}_N) \equiv f(\mathbf{p}_{\pi_1}, \ldots, \mathbf{p}_{\pi_N})$$
for any permutation $\pi$.

PointNet approximates this using a symmetric function (max pooling):
$$f(\{\mathbf{p}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{p}_N\}) = \gamma \circ g\bigl(h(\mathbf{p}_1), \ldots, h(\mathbf{p}_N)\bigr)$$

$h$: shared MLP applied independently to each point (maps $\mathbb{R}^3 \to \mathbb{R}^{1024}$)
$g$: element-wise max pooling over all points → global feature $\in \mathbb{R}^{1024}$
$\gamma$: classification MLP

sections.heading: T-Net: Learnable Alignment Module
sections.text: PointNet applies a mini-network (T-Net) to predict a transformation matrix that aligns the input point cloud to a canonical orientation before processing. The T-Net produces a $3\times3$ transformation matrix applied to the raw coordinates. A second T-Net predicts a $64\times64$ feature-space transformation. The key constraint: a regularisation term $\|TT^\top - I\|^2$ is added to the loss to keep the feature transformation close to orthogonal, ensuring the transformation doesn't distort the feature space. This makes PointNet robust to rotations and translations — two common sources of variation in 3D data.

sections.heading: PointNet for Segmentation
sections.text: For per-point segmentation, PointNet concatenates the global feature (from max pooling) with the local point feature (from the first 64-dim shared MLP) for each point. This gives each point access to both global shape context and its own local features. The combined 1088-dim vector (1024 global + 64 local) is processed by another shared MLP to predict per-point class scores. The key insight: global max pooling creates a 'summary' of the entire shape that is rebroadcast to every point, enabling each point to reason about its role within the global structure.

ida09.title: PointNet++: Hierarchical Feature Learning
ida09.summary: PointNet++ (Qi et al. 2017) applies PointNet hierarchically using a Set Abstraction module that sub-samples, groups, and extracts features at multiple scales. This builds local context at fine scales and global context at coarse scales, dramatically improving performance on complex shapes.

sections.heading: Set Abstraction Module
sections.text: Each Set Abstraction (SA) level has three components: (1) Sampling: use Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) to select $M$ representative points that cover the shape uniformly. (2) Grouping: for each representative point, find all points within radius $r$ (ball query, up to $K$ points). (3) PointNet: apply PointNet to each local group to extract a feature vector. The output is $M$ points, each with a local feature descriptor. Stacking SA levels progressively reduces the number of points ($N → M_1 → M_2$) while increasing feature dimensionality — exactly analogous to strided convolutions in CNNs building a spatial hierarchy.

sections.heading: Multi-Scale Grouping (MSG)
sections.text:
To handle non-uniform density, PointNet++ applies Multiple Scale Grouping:

For each centroid point $c$, apply ball query at radii $r_1 < r_2 < r_3$, extract PointNet features at each scale, then concatenate:
$$f(c) = [\mathrm{PN}(\mathcal{N}_{r_1}(c)),\; \mathrm{PN}(\mathcal{N}_{r_2}(c)),\; \mathrm{PN}(\mathcal{N}_{r_3}(c))]$$

Small radius: fine-grained local geometry. Large radius: coarser neighbourhood, robust to sparse regions. Concatenation provides multi-scale context at each point.

sections.heading: Feature Propagation for Segmentation
sections.text: For per-point segmentation (predicting a label for each of the $N$ original points), PointNet++ uses a Feature Propagation (FP) module to 'upsample' features back to the original resolution. Since sub-sampled points are a strict subset of the original, features are interpolated by inverse-distance weighted k-NN interpolation from the $M$ sub-sampled points to the $N$ original points. Skip connections (analogous to U-Net) pass features from the corresponding SA level directly to the FP module at the same resolution. This mirrors the encoder-decoder pattern of U-Net, achieving precise per-point predictions.

ida10.title: Autoencoders and Variational Autoencoders
ida10.summary: Autoencoders learn compact latent representations by compressing and reconstructing data. VAEs (Kingma & Welling, 2013) add a probabilistic constraint — the latent space must follow a Gaussian prior — enabling generative sampling. They are trained with the ELBO (Evidence Lower BOund).

sections.heading: VAE Loss: Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO)
sections.text:
Encoder: $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$ outputs mean $\boldsymbol{\mu}$ and variance $\boldsymbol{\sigma}^2$.
Reparameterization: $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\sigma} \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon},\; \boldsymbol{\epsilon} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$

Decoder: $p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})$ reconstructs the input.

$$\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{ELBO}} = \underbrace{\mathbb{E}_{q_\phi}[\log p_\theta(\mathbf{x}|\mathbf{z})]}_{{\text{reconstruction term}}} - \underbrace{D_{\mathrm{KL}}(q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x}) \| p(\mathbf{z}))}_{{\text{regularisation term}}}$$

For Gaussian prior $p(\mathbf{z}) = \mathcal{N}(0,I)$:
$$D_{\mathrm{KL}} = -\frac{1}{2}\sum_j (1 + \log\sigma_j^2 - \mu_j^2 - \sigma_j^2)$$

sections.heading: Why the KL Regularisation Term?
sections.text: Without regularisation, the encoder would map each input to a tiny Gaussian (small variance) around a unique point in latent space — effectively memorising, not learning a generalisable representation. The KL term forces the posterior $q_\phi(\mathbf{z}|\mathbf{x})$ to be close to the standard Gaussian prior, encouraging the latent space to be compact and smooth. In the latent space of a well-trained VAE, interpolating between two points $\mathbf{z}_1$ and $\mathbf{z}_2$ produces smooth, realistic-looking images. This smoothness is what enables generation: sample $\mathbf{z} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$ and decode it. The reparameterization trick makes this differentiable — $\mathbf{z} = \boldsymbol{\mu} + \boldsymbol{\sigma} \odot \boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ lets gradients flow through the sampling step.

ida10.title: GAN Fundamentals: Minimax Training
ida10.summary: GANs (Goodfellow et al. 2014) pit two networks against each other: a generator G produces fake images; a discriminator D tries to distinguish real from fake. Training is a minimax game that, in theory, drives the generator distribution to match the real data distribution.

sections.heading: GAN Minimax Objective
sections.text:
$$\min_G \max_D \; V(G,D) = \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{x}\sim p_{\mathrm{data}}}[\log D(\mathbf{x})] + \mathbb{E}_{\mathbf{z}\sim p_\mathbf{z}}[\log(1-D(G(\mathbf{z})))]$$

$D$ maximises: correctly classify real as 1, fake as 0.
$G$ minimises: make D classify G(z) as real (close to 1).

In practice, $G$ maximises $\mathbb{E}[\log D(G(\mathbf{z}))]$ (non-saturating loss) instead, which provides stronger gradients early in training when D is confident that G's outputs are fake.

sections.heading: Mode Collapse and Training Instability
sections.text: GAN training is notoriously unstable. Mode collapse: G finds a small number of outputs that fool D and keeps generating only those, ignoring diversity (e.g. generating only one digit regardless of input). Training oscillations: D becomes too strong → G gets no gradient signal (D(G(z)) ≈ 0 → log(1-0) = 0, flat); D becomes too weak → G doesn't need to improve. Wasserstein GAN (WGAN) reformulates the objective as the Wasserstein-1 distance, providing meaningful gradients even when distributions don't overlap, solving many instability issues. Spectral normalisation (SN-GAN) constrains the discriminator's Lipschitz constant, stabilising training.

left.title: GAN
points: Mode collapse risk — may miss modes of p_data

right.title: VAE
points: Tractable ELBO (lower bound on log p(x))
Better latent space structure for editing

ida10.title: Conditional GANs and Image-to-Image Translation
ida10.summary: Conditional GANs (cGANs) condition both G and D on additional information (class label, input image), enabling controlled synthesis. Pix2pix enables paired image-to-image translation; CycleGAN enables unpaired translation using cycle-consistency.

sections.heading: Pix2pix: Paired Image Translation
sections.text: Pix2pix (Isola et al. 2017) trains a cGAN with paired training data: input image $x$ (e.g. semantic label map) and target image $y$ (e.g. photo). The generator is a U-Net that maps $x → y$. The discriminator is a PatchGAN that classifies whether overlapping $70\times70$ image patches are real or fake — rather than classifying the entire image at once. PatchGAN is more efficient and focuses the discriminator on local texture quality. The loss is $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}_{cGAN} + \lambda \mathcal{L}_{L1}$, where the L1 term directly encourages low-frequency similarity to the target (handles large-scale structure) while the adversarial term encourages high-frequency detail.

sections.heading: CycleGAN: Unpaired Translation
sections.text: CycleGAN (Zhu et al. 2017) enables translation between two image domains X and Y without paired examples (e.g. horses↔zebras, photos↔Monet paintings). Two generators: $G: X → Y$ and $F: Y → X$, with two discriminators. Cycle-consistency loss enforces that translating back and forth should preserve the original: $\|F(G(x)) - x\|_1 + \|G(F(y)) - y\|_1$. Without pairing, the only supervision is this cycle-consistency — the network must learn a mapping that is both convincing to the discriminator and reversible. This prevents degenerate solutions (e.g. just ignoring the input and generating any image from domain Y).

ida11.title: Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models: Overview
ida11.summary: DDPMs (Ho et al. 2020) define a Markov chain that gradually adds Gaussian noise to data (forward process) and learn to reverse it (backward process). Unlike GANs, training is stable; unlike VAEs, the latent space has the same dimensionality as the data. They currently produce the best image quality.

left.title: GANs / VAEs
points: GAN: unstable adversarial training, mode collapse
VAE: blurry outputs, surrogate ELBO loss

right.title: Diffusion Models (DDPM)
points: Stable training: simple noise prediction loss
Latent space same dim as data
Slow: T=1000 denoising steps for generation

sections.heading: Key Idea: Destroy then Reconstruct
sections.text: Diffusion models are inspired by non-equilibrium thermodynamics. The forward process gradually destroys the data structure by adding noise, until the image becomes pure Gaussian noise. The model learns the reverse: given a slightly noisier image, predict the slightly less noisy version. At generation time, start from pure Gaussian noise and apply the learned reverse process T times to produce a sample from the data distribution. The training signal comes from comparing the predicted noise at each step to the actual noise that was added — a simple regression loss. This stability (no discriminator, no mode collapse) is the key advantage over GANs.

ida11.title: Forward Diffusion: Markov Chain and Reparameterisation
ida11.summary: The forward process is a fixed Markov chain that adds Gaussian noise with variance β_t at each step. The reparameterisation trick collapses this to a closed-form expression, allowing direct sampling of x_t at any timestep from x_0 without running T sequential steps.

sections.heading: Forward Process: Step-by-Step and Closed Form
sections.text:
Step-wise forward process:
$$q(\mathbf{x}_t | \mathbf{x}_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t;\; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,\mathbf{x}_{t-1},\; \beta_t \mathbf{I})$$

Full trajectory (Markov):
$$q(\mathbf{x}_{1:T}|\mathbf{x}_0) = \prod_{t=1}^T q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_{t-1})$$

Let $\alpha_t = 1-\beta_t$, $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=0}^t \alpha_s$. **Reparameterisation** (closed form, sample any $t$ directly):
$$\mathbf{x}_t \sim q(\mathbf{x}_t|\mathbf{x}_0) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_t;\; \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{x}_0,\; (1-\bar{\alpha}_t)\mathbf{I})$$
$$\mathbf{x}_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\mathbf{x}_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\boldsymbol{\epsilon},\quad \boldsymbol{\epsilon}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I)$$

sections.heading: Variance Schedule: Linear vs Cosine
sections.text: The variance schedule $\{\beta_t\}_{t=1}^T$ controls how fast information is destroyed. Ho et al. (2020) used a linear schedule: $\beta_1 = 10^{-4}$ increasing to $\beta_T = 0.02$ over $T=1000$ steps. The linear schedule destroys information quickly — $\bar{\alpha}_t$ falls rapidly in the early steps, wasting many denoising steps on already-noisy data. Nichol et al. (2021) proposed a cosine schedule: $\bar{\alpha}_t = \cos^2(\frac{\pi}{2}\frac{t/T+s}{1+s})$, which decreases more slowly and smoothly, keeping structure longer at moderate noise levels. The cosine schedule significantly improves sample quality.

ida11.title: Reverse Diffusion and Training
ida11.summary: The reverse process is learned by a neural network (U-Net with time conditioning) that predicts the noise added at each step. Training minimises a simple MSE loss between predicted and actual noise. The U-Net architecture with temporal embedding enables high-quality denoising.
ida11.context: Lecture 11 · 11.01.2024 — Learning the reverse diffusion process

sections.heading: Reverse Process and Training Objective
sections.text:
Reverse process (parameterised by neural net $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta$):
$$p_\theta(\mathbf{x}_{t-1}|\mathbf{x}_t) = \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{x}_{t-1};\; \boldsymbol{\mu}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t),\; \sigma_t^2 \mathbf{I})$$

Simplified training objective (Ho et al.):
$$\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{simple}} = \mathbb{E}_{t, \mathbf{x}_0, \boldsymbol{\epsilon}}\bigl[\| \boldsymbol{\epsilon} - \boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t) \|^2\bigr]$$

The network $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta$ predicts the noise $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}$ that was added to $\mathbf{x}_0$ to produce $\mathbf{x}_t$.

Sampling (DDPM): $\mathbf{x}_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}\bigl(\mathbf{x}_t - \frac{1-\alpha_t}{\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}}\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t,t)\bigr) + \sigma_t\mathbf{z}$

sections.heading: U-Net Architecture for Noise Prediction
sections.text: The noise prediction network $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta$ is a U-Net with residual connections and attention at multiple resolutions. The timestep $t$ is encoded via sinusoidal positional encoding (analogous to Transformer positional encoding) and added to the feature maps at each resolution via scale-shift normalisation (AdaGN). This allows the same network to handle all timesteps — at small $t$ (slightly noisy), the network learns fine detail denoising; at large $t$ (heavily noisy), it learns coarse structure recovery. DDPM inference requires $T=1000$ sequential steps, which is slow. DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models) achieves comparable quality in 50–200 steps by using a deterministic sampling process.

ida11.title: Conditional Diffusion and Text-to-Image Generation
ida11.summary: Classifier-free guidance conditions the diffusion model on a signal (text, class, image) without needing a separate classifier. Combined with latent diffusion (compressing to a lower-dimensional space first), this enables practical text-to-image models like Stable Diffusion and DALL-E 2.
ida11.context: Lecture 11 · 11.01.2024 — Conditional generation and modern applications

sections.heading: Classifier-Free Guidance
sections.text:
Train a single conditional model $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, t, c)$ and occasionally drop the condition $c$ (replace with null token $\emptyset$) during training at probability $p_{\mathrm{uncond}}$ (typically 0.1–0.2).

At sampling time, interpolate between unconditional and conditional predictions:
$$\hat{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, c) = (1+w)\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, c) - w\boldsymbol{\epsilon}_\theta(\mathbf{x}_t, \emptyset)$$

$w > 0$: guidance strength. Large $w$ → images more strongly conditioned on $c$ (sharper, more faithful to prompt) but less diverse. $w=0$: unconditional. Typical: $w=7.5$.

sections.heading: Stable Diffusion: Latent Diffusion Models
sections.text: Running diffusion in pixel space on a 512×512 image is expensive — $T=1000$ U-Net passes on a large image. Stable Diffusion (Rombach et al. 2022) uses Latent Diffusion Models (LDM): a VQ-VAE first encodes the image to a compact latent representation (8× spatially smaller: 512×512 → 64×64), and the diffusion process runs in this latent space. The U-Net runs on 64×64 latents, dramatically reducing compute. Text conditioning uses CLIP's text encoder — the text embedding is injected into the U-Net via cross-attention at each resolution. At generation time: diffuse in latent space, then decode with the VAE decoder.

ida12.title: Fully-Convolutional Point Network (FCPN)
ida12.summary: FCPN (Rethage et al. 2018) is a hybrid point-to-voxel architecture: PointNet maps raw points to a regular 3D voxel grid, then standard 3D convolutions process the resulting dense feature volume. This enables processing arbitrarily large point clouds in a single forward pass.

sections.heading: Motivation: Limitations of PointNet++
sections.text: PointNet++ uses hierarchical sampling and grouping to process large point clouds in tiles, stitching together overlapping results — creating redundant computation and boundary artefacts. FCPN proposes a fundamentally different approach: use a simplified PointNet only to go from the irregular point cloud to a regular 3D grid of features (the 'point-to-voxel' step), then process this regular grid with standard 3D convolutions and 3D deconvolutions (analogous to a 3D U-Net). The voxelisation step makes the problem regular, enabling all the optimisations of standard CNNs (GPU batch convolutions, depthwise separable conv, etc.) while still accepting raw unordered point clouds as input.

sections.heading: Architecture and Applications
sections.text: FCPN architecture: (1) A simplified PointNet (MLP + max pool within each voxel cell) aggregates points falling into each voxel, producing a regular 3D feature volume. (2) 3D convolutions at multiple scales (1×1×1, 3×3×3) process the volume with encoder-decoder skip connections. (3) Three parallel output heads: (a) 3D deconvolution → voxel completion, (b) Latent nearest-neighbour interpolation → dense per-point features, (c) Fully-connected → global classification. Applications demonstrated: semantic segmentation of large-scale indoor scenes (up to 200k points), scene captioning ('You are in a sleeping area'). The network can process volumes of $2.4m^3$ trained on $2.4m^3$ cubes without tiling.

ida12.title: Dynamic Graph CNN and EdgeConv
ida12.summary: DGCNN (Wang et al. 2019) introduces EdgeConv — a graph convolution that explicitly computes edge features between a point and its k-NN neighbours. The graph is dynamically recomputed at each layer in feature space, enabling the network to capture both local geometric and global semantic relationships.

sections.heading: EdgeConv Operation
sections.text:
For point $\mathbf{x}_i$ and its $k$-NN $\{\mathbf{x}_{j_1},\ldots,\mathbf{x}_{j_k}\}$:

Edge feature: $e_{ij} = h_\theta(\mathbf{x}_i,\; \mathbf{x}_{ij})$ where $\mathbf{x}_{ij} = \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_i$ (relative position)

Typically: $e_{ij} = \mathrm{MLP}([\mathbf{x}_i,\; \mathbf{x}_j - \mathbf{x}_i])$ concatenating the point itself and the relative offset.

Aggregation via symmetric function (max or mean):
$$\mathbf{x}_i' = \max_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} e_{ij}$$

Key: the $k$-NN graph is recomputed **in feature space** at each layer, not fixed in 3D space.

sections.heading: Why Dynamic Graphs in Feature Space?
sections.text: In early layers, DGCNN uses k-NN in the input 3D coordinate space — points that are geometrically close are grouped together. After the first EdgeConv, feature vectors are updated. In subsequent layers, k-NN is computed in the new feature space — points that are semantically similar (e.g., all wing points of an airplane, regardless of their 3D positions) become neighbours. This allows the network to learn relations that are semantically meaningful but not spatially local. The visualised effect: in deep layers, the groups of k-NN neighbours show semantically coherent structures (nose of airplane grouped with nose of another airplane) even across large geometric distances.

ida12.title: FoldingNet and DDPMs for Point Clouds
ida12.summary: FoldingNet is an autoencoder that represents a point cloud by 'unfolding' a 2D grid into 3D, learning compact latent codes. DDPMs for point clouds apply the diffusion framework to generate 3D shapes, enabling shape completion and unconditional generation.
ida12.context: Lecture 12 · 18.01.2024 — Generative models for point clouds

sections.heading: FoldingNet: 2D Grid Folding Decoder
sections.text: FoldingNet (Yang et al. 2018) is an autoencoder where the encoder is a graph-based PointNet that produces a global codeword $c \in \mathbb{R}^{512}$. The decoder is the key innovation: it takes a fixed 2D grid of points $(u,v)$ and the codeword $c$, and concatenates them: $[u, v, c]$. A 3-layer MLP then maps this to 3D coordinates $(x,y,z)$. The decoder effectively 'folds' a flat 2D grid into the 3D surface, like folding a sheet of paper into a 3D shape. Two folding steps (two MLPs chained) allow complex deformations. The key insight: most 3D surfaces are locally 2-manifolds (like crumpled paper), so a 2D parameterisation is natural.

sections.heading: DDPM for 3D Point Cloud Generation
sections.text: Applying DDPMs to point clouds treats the point cloud as a set of 3D coordinates and defines the forward process as gradually adding Gaussian noise to all point positions until they become a standard Gaussian distribution (isotropic noise in 3D). The reverse process predicts the noise added to each point at each step. The denoising network takes the noisy point cloud + timestep and predicts the noise, using PointNet++ or DGCNN as the backbone. Conditioning on a partial point cloud enables shape completion: start from noise, condition on the known partial shape, and generate the missing parts. This achieves high-quality completion with better diversity than deterministic methods.

ida13.title: Bag of Visual Words
ida13.summary: Bag of Visual Words (BoVW) adapts the NLP bag-of-words model to images. Local image descriptors (SIFT, SURF) are clustered into a visual vocabulary. Each image is represented as a histogram of visual word frequencies — a fixed-length descriptor suitable for SVM classification.
ida13.context: Lecture 13 · 25.01.2024 — Bag of Visual Words and CNN Visualization

sections.heading: The Four-Step BoVW Pipeline
sections.text: Step 1 — Feature Detection: select image patches using interest point detectors (SIFT, Harris) or dense regular-grid sampling. Step 2 — Feature Description: compute a descriptor for each patch (SIFT: 128-dim gradient histogram; SURF: 64-dim Haar wavelet responses). These capture local appearance invariant to scale and rotation. Step 3 — Vocabulary Construction: cluster all descriptors from all training images using K-means (K = 500–10000 visual words). Each cluster centre is a 'visual word'. Step 4 — Image Encoding: for each image, assign each local descriptor to its nearest visual word and count frequencies. The K-dimensional histogram of visual word counts is the image representation. Train a classifier (SVM) on these histograms.

sections.heading: TF-IDF Weighting for Visual Words
sections.text:
Raw frequency counts give too much weight to common visual words (edges, flat regions). TF-IDF (Term Frequency — Inverse Document Frequency) down-weights frequent words:

$$w_{ij} = \underbrace{\frac{n_{ij}}{\sum_k n_{ik}}}_{\text{TF}} \cdot \underbrace{\log\frac{N}{n_j}}_{\text{IDF}}$$

$n_{ij}$: count of visual word $j$ in image $i$; $n_j$: number of images containing word $j$; $N$: total images.

Words appearing in most images (low IDF) → down-weighted. Words appearing rarely (high IDF) → up-weighted (more discriminative).

right.title: CNN Features
points: Transferable via fine-tuning to new tasks

ida13.title: CNN Visualization: Understanding What Networks Learn
ida13.summary: CNN visualisation methods reveal what features each layer and filter has learned. Techniques range from direct filter visualisation to activation maximisation (what input maximises a neuron?) and occlusion sensitivity maps (what image region matters for the prediction?).

sections.heading: ZFNet: Deconvnet Approach
sections.text: Zeiler & Fergus (2014) introduced the Deconvolutional Network (Deconvnet) to visualise CNN features. Given an activation in some layer, the Deconvnet maps it back to the input pixel space by: (1) unpooling: remember the max-pool locations (switch maps) and put the activation back at those locations; (2) rectification: apply ReLU (only keep positive values); (3) deconvolution: transpose convolution with the transposed filter. By doing this layer by layer from the chosen unit back to the input, they visualised which input patterns maximally excite each filter. Observation: early layers learn edge detectors (Gabor-like), mid layers learn textures and object parts, deep layers learn entire objects.

sections.heading: Activation Maximisation and Saliency Maps
sections.text: Activation maximisation: find the input image $\mathbf{x}^* = \arg\max_\mathbf{x} a_j(\mathbf{x}) - \lambda \|\mathbf{x}\|^2$ that maximally activates unit $j$ in layer $k$, via gradient ascent in input space. Without regularisation, these produce adversarial noise patterns. With regularisation (L2, total variation, natural image prior), they reveal interpretable features. Gradient saliency (Simonyan et al.): compute $|\partial f_c / \partial \mathbf{x}|$ — the gradient of the class score with respect to input pixels. High gradient → pixel contributes more to the prediction. GradCAM improves this by weighting class activation maps with the class-specific gradient.

sections.heading: GradCAM: Gradient-Weighted Class Activation Maps
sections.text:
For class $c$ and the last convolutional layer with feature maps $A^k \in \mathbb{R}^{H\times W}$:

**Importance weights**:
$$\alpha_k^c = \frac{1}{HW}\sum_{i,j} \frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}} \quad \text{(global average pool of gradients)}$$

**Class Activation Map**:
$$L^c = \mathrm{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_k \alpha_k^c A^k\right)$$

ReLU: keep only features that positively influence class $c$. Upsample $L^c$ to input resolution and overlay on image → heatmap showing which image regions the network 'looks at' for class $c$.

ida13.title: t-SNE: Visualising High-Dimensional Data
ida13.summary: t-SNE (van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008) embeds high-dimensional data into 2D for visualisation, preserving local neighbourhood structure. It is the standard tool for exploring and validating learned feature spaces — essential for understanding CNN representations.
ida13.context: Lecture 13 · 25.01.2024 — Visualizing feature spaces with t-SNE

sections.heading: t-SNE Objective
sections.text:
High-dim similarities (Gaussian kernel):
$$p_{j|i} = \frac{\exp(-\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)}{\sum_{k\neq i}\exp(-\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_k\|^2 / 2\sigma_i^2)},\quad p_{ij} = \frac{p_{j|i}+p_{i|j}}{2N}$$

Low-dim similarities (Student t-distribution, $\nu=1$):
$$q_{ij} = \frac{(1+\|\mathbf{y}_i - \mathbf{y}_j\|^2)^{-1}}{\sum_{k\neq l}(1+\|\mathbf{y}_k - \mathbf{y}_l\|^2)^{-1}}$$

Minimise KL divergence: $\mathcal{L} = \mathrm{KL}(P \| Q) = \sum_{ij} p_{ij} \log \frac{p_{ij}}{q_{ij}}$

sections.heading: Why the t-Distribution?
sections.text: SNE (the original version) used Gaussian kernels in both high- and low-dim spaces. The problem: in 2D, there is far less 'space' than in 100D — many points that are moderately distant in high-dim must all map to a crowded 2D region. A Gaussian kernel requires very small low-dim distances to represent moderate high-dim similarities, causing the 'crowding problem' — clusters are compressed together. Using a Student t-distribution (heavy tails) in 2D allows moderately similar high-dim points to be mapped to much larger low-dim distances — resolving the crowding by spreading clusters apart. This is why t-SNE produces much cleaner cluster separation than PCA or standard SNE.

sections.heading: t-SNE for Evaluating CNN Features
sections.text: A standard validation step for image analysis models: extract CNN features for a test set, run t-SNE to 2D, and colour points by class label. Well-trained models produce clearly separated clusters. If clusters overlap or are mixed, the features are not sufficiently discriminative. Common finding: early CNN layers → features from the same class spread across many clusters (texture-based grouping); final layers → class-based clustering dominates. DINO (self-supervised ViT) features show remarkably clean class separation even without supervision — a striking validation of the quality of self-supervised visual representations.

py01.title: print()
py01.summary: Stdout'a yazar. sep argümanlar arası, end son argüman sonrası.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: print(*args, sep=' ', end='\n', file=sys.stdout, flush=False)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Stdout'a yazar. sep argümanlar arası, end son argüman sonrası.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
print('Hello', 'World')        # Hello World
print(1, 2, 3, sep=', ')       # 1, 2, 3
print('no newline', end=' ')   # satır sonu yok
print(f'x={x:.2f}')            # f-string ile

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: flush=True real-time output için (progress bar gibi). file=sys.stderr ile stderr'e yazar.

py01.title: input()
py01.summary: stdin'den satır okur. Her zaman string döner.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: val = input('prompt: ')

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: stdin'den satır okur. Her zaman string döner.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
name = input('Name: ')             # str
age  = int(input('Age: '))         # int'e çevir
x, y = map(int, input().split())   # iki int al

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Her zaman str döner, explicit çevrim gerek. map(int, input().split()) yaygın pattern.

py01.title: type() / isinstance()
py01.summary: type(): tam tipi verir. isinstance(): inheritance'ı da kontrol eder, tercih edilen yol.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: type(x)  |  isinstance(x, type_or_tuple)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: type(): tam tipi verir. isinstance(): inheritance'ı da kontrol eder, tercih edilen yol.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
type(42)                      # <class 'int'>
type('hi') == str             # True
isinstance(42, int)           # True
isinstance(3.14, (int,float)) # True (tuple ile OR)
isinstance(True, int)         # True! (bool int'ten türer)

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: isinstance() kullan, type() == değil. bool int subclass'ıdır.

py01.title: range()
py01.summary: Lazy integer dizisi üretir. List'e çevirmeden iterasyon için ideal.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: range(stop)  |  range(start, stop, step)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Lazy integer dizisi üretir. List'e çevirmeden iterasyon için ideal.

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: O(1) space. `x in range(n)` O(1)'dir, list'te O(n). Geriye sayım için step=-1.

py01.title: enumerate()
py01.summary: (index, value) çiftleri döner. range(len(lst)) kullanmaya gerek kalmaz.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: enumerate(iterable, start=0)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: (index, value) çiftleri döner. range(len(lst)) kullanmaya gerek kalmaz.

sections.heading: Örnek
Notlar
sections.text: range(len(lst)) yerine hep enumerate kullan. start parametresi 1-indexed sayım için.

py01.title: Unpacking / *rest
py01.summary: Extended unpacking ile fazla elemanları yakala. Tek satırda swap.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
a, b, *rest = iterable
x, y = y, x

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Extended unpacking ile fazla elemanları yakala. Tek satırda swap.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
a, b, c = [1, 2, 3]
first, *rest = [1,2,3,4]    # first=1, rest=[2,3,4]
*init, last  = [1,2,3,4]    # init=[1,2,3], last=4
a, *_, z = range(10)        # a=0, z=9 (orta atlanır)
x, y = y, x                 # swap (temp yok)

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: * assignment'ta her zaman list yaratır, boş bile: a, *b = [1] → b=[].

py01.title: Walrus Operator :=
py01.summary: Assignment expression. Assign ederken aynı zamanda değeri döner. Python 3.8+

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: name := expr

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Assignment expression. Assign ederken aynı zamanda değeri döner. Python 3.8+

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
# while loop:
while chunk := f.read(1024):
    process(chunk)

# if:
if m := re.search(pattern, text):
    print(m.group())

# comprehension:
results = [y for x in data if (y := f(x)) > 0]

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Normal atama ile karıştırma: := sadece expression context'te. Özellikle while/if'te kullanışlı.

py01.title: Operatörler & Tip Dönüşümü
py01.summary: Python'da özel operatörler ve tip dönüşümleri. `==` değer eşitliği, `is` nesne kimliği.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: //  %  **  ==  is  int()  float()  str()

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Python'da özel operatörler ve tip dönüşümleri. `==` değer eşitliği, `is` nesne kimliği.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
10 // 3          # 3   (floor division)
10 % 3           # 1   (modulo)
2 ** 8           # 256 (kuvvet)
2 == 2.0         # True  (değer eşit)
2 is 2.0         # False (farklı nesne)
x = 3.7
int(x)           # 3   (kesmek)
float('3.14')    # 3.14
str(42)          # '42'
a = b = c = 0    # çoklu atama
x, y, z = 1,2,3  # tuple unpacking ile

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: `is` None kontrolü için kullan: `if x is None`. Sayılar için `==` kullan. `is` small int'lerde True görünebilir (CPython cache) ama güvenme.

py01.title: Tuple
py01.summary: Immutable, sıralı koleksiyon. List gibi ama değiştirilemez. Heterogeneous (farklı tipler) olabilir.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: t = (a, b, c)  |  t = a, b, c  |  t = (x,)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Immutable, sıralı koleksiyon. List gibi ama değiştirilemez. Heterogeneous (farklı tipler) olabilir.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
t = (1, 'hello', 3.14)  # heterojen
t[0]                    # 1  (indexing
t[-1]                   # 3.14
x, y, z = t             # unpacking
a, b = b, a             # swap (tuple ile)

# Tek elemanlı tuple — virgül şart!
tek = (3,)              # tuple
tek = (3)               # int! (parantez)

# Tuple vs List:
tuple([1,2,3])          # list → tuple
list((1,2,3))           # tuple → list

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Tuple'lar dict key olabilir (hashable), list olamaz. Virgülsüz `(x)` sadece parantez, tuple değil.

py01.title: Modules & Import
py01.summary: Modül ve paket import yolları. Her modül bir kez yüklenir. `__name__` ile script/modül ayrımı yapılır.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
import x  |  import x as y
from x import y  |  from x.y import z

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Modül ve paket import yolları. Her modül bir kez yüklenir. `__name__` ile script/modül ayrımı yapılır.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
import numpy                      # numpy.array()
import numpy as np                # np.array() — standart alias
from numpy import array, zeros    # direkt isimle
from numpy import array as arr    # alias ile

# Paket içinden:
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
from collections import defaultdict, Counter

# __name__ pattern — script vs modül:
def main():
    print('Çalışıyor')

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()   # sadece `python dosya.py` ile çalışırken
             # import edilince çalışmaz

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: `from x import *` kullanma — namespace kirlenir. Modül değişince Jupyter'da kernel restart gerekir (bir kez yüklenir).

py01.title: pass / break / continue
py01.summary: Kontrol akışı anahtar kelimeleri. pass: boş blok. break: döngüden çık. continue: sonraki iterasyona atla.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: pass  |  break  |  continue

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Kontrol akışı anahtar kelimeleri. pass: boş blok. break: döngüden çık. continue: sonraki iterasyona atla.

sections.heading: Örnek
Notlar
sections.text: `for...else` ve `while...else` — break ile çıkılmadıysa else çalışır. Nadir ama kullanışlı.

py01.title: if / elif / else
py01.summary: Koşullu dallanma. Chained karşılaştırma, short-circuit, truthy/falsy ve Python 3.10+ match-case destekler.

sections.heading: Syntax
Açıklama
sections.text: Koşullu dallanma. Chained karşılaştırma, short-circuit, truthy/falsy ve Python 3.10+ match-case destekler.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
x = 42
if x > 100:   print('büyük')
elif x > 10:  print('orta')
else:         print('küçük')

# Chained — matematiksel notation
if 0 <= x < 100:          # 0 ≤ x < 100
    print('aralıkta')

# Ternary
result = 'pos' if x > 0 else 'neg'
grade  = 'A' if s>=90 else 'B' if s>=80 else 'C'

# Short-circuit: and/or
if name and name.startswith('A'):  # name boşsa 2. part çalışmaz
    print('A ile başlıyor')
default = user_input or 'anonim'   # falsy ise sağ taraf

# Truthy / Falsy
if lst:        # [] → False, doluysa True
if d:          # {} → False
if x is None:  # None kontrolü — == None değil!
if not errors: # boşsa True

# match-case (Python 3.10+) — structural pattern matching
point = (1, 0)
match point:
    case (0, 0):        print('Origin')
    case (x, 0):        print(f'x ekseninde x={x}')
    case (0, y):        print(f'y ekseninde y={y}')
    case (x, y):        print(f'({x}, {y})')
    case _:             print('diğer')

match command:
    case 'quit' | 'exit': sys.exit()
    case str(s) if s.startswith('go'): move(s)
    case _: print('bilinmeyen komut')

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Falsy: None, 0, 0.0, '', [], {}, set(), (). Short-circuit: `a and b` → a False ise b hiç eval edilmez, `a or b` → a True ise b atlanır. match-case if-elif zincirinden daha performanslı ve okunabilir.

py01.title: for Döngüsü
py01.summary: Herhangi bir iterable üzerinde döner. enumerate, zip, nested, reversed, sorted ve for…else ile çok yönlü.

sections.heading: Syntax
Açıklama
sections.text: Herhangi bir iterable üzerinde döner. enumerate, zip, nested, reversed, sorted ve for…else ile çok yönlü.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
# Temel
for i in range(5):            # 0,1,2,3,4
for c in 'hello':             # karakter iter
for k, v in d.items():        # dict key-value

# İndeks + değer
for i, v in enumerate(lst):   # range(len(lst)) değil!
for i, v in enumerate(lst, start=1):  # 1-indexed

# Paralel iterasyon
for a, b in zip(l1, l2):      # en kısa olanda durur
for a, b, c in zip(l1, l2, l3):

# Sıralı / ters
for x in sorted(lst, key=len):     # sıralı
for x in reversed(lst):            # ters (in-place değil)
for k in sorted(d, reverse=True):  # dict key sıralı

# Nested loops
for i in range(3):
    for j in range(3):
        print(i, j)           # 9 satır

# for…else — break olmadıysa else çalışır
for x in lst:
    if x == target:
        print('bulundu')
        break
else:
    print('bulunamadı')       # break yoksa

# Dikkat: loop sırasında listeyi değiştirme!
for x in lst[:]:              # kopya üzerinde dön
    if bad(x): lst.remove(x)

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: range(len(lst)) yerine hep enumerate kullan. Zip eşit uzunluk bekler; farklı uzunluklar için itertools.zip_longest. reversed() iterator döner — list(reversed(lst)) ile kopyala.

py01.title: while Döngüsü
py01.summary: Koşul True olduğu sürece döner. Sonsuz döngü veya koşul önceden bilinmeyenler için kullanılır.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
while condition:
    ...

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Koşul True olduğu sürece döner. Sonsuz döngü veya koşul önceden bilinmeyenler için kullanılır.

sections.heading: Örnek
Notlar
sections.text: Sonsuz döngüden çıkmak için break veya koşul değişikliği şart. Sayaçlı iterasyon için for + range tercih et.

py01.title: list.append() / extend()
py01.summary: append: tek eleman ekler. extend: iterable'daki tüm elemanları ekler.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: lst.append(x)  |  lst.extend(iterable)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: append: tek eleman ekler. extend: iterable'daki tüm elemanları ekler.

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: append([x]) listeyi iç içe ekler. extend([x]) düzleştirir. += in-place değiştirir (id aynı kalır).

py01.title: list.insert() / remove() / pop()
py01.summary: insert: indekse ekle. remove: ilk eşleşeni sil. pop: indeksteki elemanı çıkar ve döner.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: lst.insert(i, x)  |  lst.remove(x)  |  lst.pop(i=-1)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: insert: indekse ekle. remove: ilk eşleşeni sil. pop: indeksteki elemanı çıkar ve döner.

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: pop() sondan → O(1). pop(i) ortadan → O(n). remove() bulamazsa ValueError.

py01.title: list.sort() / sorted()
py01.summary: sort(): in-place, None döner. sorted(): yeni list döner. İkisi de Timsort kullanır.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
lst.sort(key=None, reverse=False)
sorted(iterable, key=None, reverse=False)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: sort(): in-place, None döner. sorted(): yeni list döner. İkisi de Timsort kullanır.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
nums = [3,1,4,1,5]
nums.sort()                       # [1,1,3,4,5]
nums.sort(reverse=True)           # [5,4,3,1,1]
words = ['banana','apple']
sorted(words, key=len)            # ['apple','banana']
pairs = [(1,'b'),(2,'a')]
sorted(pairs, key=lambda x: x[1]) # sıralama 2. eleman

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: key=str.lower, key=len, key=lambda x: x[1] yaygın pattern'lar. operator.itemgetter(1) lambda'dan hızlı.

py01.title: List Slicing
py01.summary: Alt liste çıkarır. Negatif indeks sondan sayar. Yeni list döner.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: lst[start:stop:step]

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Alt liste çıkarır. Negatif indeks sondan sayar. Yeni list döner.

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: a[::-1] reverse kopyası. a[:] shallow copy. Slice assignment: a[1:3] = [9,8].

py01.title: List Comprehension
py01.summary: Compact list oluşturma. for-loop + append'den hızlı. Ternary, nested, walrus, generator destekler.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
[expr for item in iter if cond]
[expr_if_true if cond else expr_if_false for ...]

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Compact list oluşturma. for-loop + append'den hızlı. Ternary, nested, walrus, generator destekler.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
# Temel
squares = [x**2 for x in range(10)]
evens   = [x for x in range(20) if x%2==0]

# Ternary (her elemana uygula, filtre değil)
abs_v = [x if x >= 0 else -x for x in nums]
grade = ['pass' if x>=50 else 'fail' for x in scores]

# Çoklu koşul (AND mantığı)
result = [x for x in range(50) if x%2==0 if x%3==0]

# Flatten (iç içe liste düzleştir)
flat = [x for row in matrix for x in row]

# String temizleme
clean = [w.strip().lower() for w in lines if w.strip()]

# Walrus := (hesaplamayı tekrarlamamak için)
results = [y for x in data if (y := f(x)) is not None]

# Matris transpose
transpose = [[row[i] for row in matrix]
             for i in range(len(matrix[0]))]

# () → generator expression (lazy, büyük veri için)
gen = (x**2 for x in range(10**6))   # O(1) bellek
sum(x**2 for x in range(1000))        # direkt kullan
any(x > 5 for x in lst)               # short-circuit

# {} → set / dict comprehension
uniq = {len(w) for w in words}
sq_d = {x: x**2 for x in range(5)}

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: 3'ten fazla for/if koyma — okunabilirlik bozulur, normal loop tercih et. Ternary ifade `if-else` her elemana uygulanır; sondaki `if` ise filtreler. Generator'u sum/any/all/max'e direkt ver, list() çevirme.

py01.title: zip()
py01.summary: Birden fazla iterable'ı çiftler. En kısa olanda durur.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: zip(*iterables)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Birden fazla iterable'ı çiftler. En kısa olanda durur.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
names  = ['Alice','Bob','Carol']
scores = [90, 85, 92]
for name, score in zip(names, scores):
    print(f'{name}: {score}')
d = dict(zip(names, scores))
a,b = zip(*[(1,'a'),(2,'b')])  # unzip

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: En kısa iterable'da durur. Eşit uzunluk için itertools.zip_longest() kullan.

py01.title: Set / Dict Comprehension
py01.summary: List comprehension'ın set ve dict versiyonları. {} parantez kullanır.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: {expr for x in iter}  |  {k: v for x in iter}

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: List comprehension'ın set ve dict versiyonları. {} parantez kullanır.

sections.heading: Örnek
Notlar
sections.text: Boş {} dict oluşturur, boş set için `set()` kullan. Okunabilirlik için çok iç içe yapmaktan kaçın.

py01.title: str.split() / join()
py01.summary: split: string → list. join: list → string. En çok kullanılan string işlemleri.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
s.split(sep=None, maxsplit=-1)
sep.join(iterable)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: split: string → list. join: list → string. En çok kullanılan string işlemleri.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
'a,b,c'.split(',')      # ['a','b','c']
'hello world'.split()   # ['hello','world'] (whitespace)
','.join(['a','b','c']) # 'a,b,c'
' '.join(words)          # 'kelime1 kelime2'
''.join(chars)           # karakterleri birleştir

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: join() loop'ta += den çok daha hızlı. sep.join(lst) şeklinde, lst.join() değil.

py01.title: str.strip() / replace()
py01.summary: strip: baş/son karakterleri siler (default: whitespace). replace: substring değiştir.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: s.strip([chars])  |  s.replace(old, new, count=-1)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: strip: baş/son karakterleri siler (default: whitespace). replace: substring değiştir.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
'  hello  '.strip()         # 'hello'
'xxhelloxx'.strip('x')     # 'hello'
'hello'.lstrip('h')        # 'ello'
'aabbcc'.replace('b','X')  # 'aaXXcc'
'aaa'.replace('a','b', 2)  # 'bba' (max 2)

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: String'ler immutable — tüm metodlar yeni string döner. strip('xy') her iki karakteri de siler.

py01.title: str.find() / count() / in
py01.summary: find: ilk eşleşmenin indeksi (-1 if not found). count: örtüşmeyen sayısı. in: üyelik testi.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: s.find(sub)  |  s.count(sub)  |  sub in s

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: find: ilk eşleşmenin indeksi (-1 if not found). count: örtüşmeyen sayısı. in: üyelik testi.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
'hello world'.find('world')  # 6
'hello'.find('xyz')          # -1  (ValueError yok)
'hello'.index('l')           # 2   (bulamazsa ValueError)
'abcabc'.count('abc')        # 2
'world' in 'hello world'     # True

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Sadece var/yok için 'in' kullan. index() bulamazsa ValueError, find() -1 döner.

py01.title: f-strings
py01.summary: Python 3.6+ inline string interpolation. Format spec'ler ve dönüşümler desteklenir.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: f'{expr!r:.2f}'

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Python 3.6+ inline string interpolation. Format spec'ler ve dönüşümler desteklenir.

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: !r=repr, !s=str, !a=ascii. Format spec: {val:width.precisiontype}. Python 3.12'de {expr=} debug için.

py01.title: str.startswith() / endswith() / upper() / lower()
py01.summary: Prefix/suffix kontrolü ve case dönüşümleri.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
s.startswith(prefix)  |  s.endswith(suffix)
s.upper()  |  s.lower()  |  s.title()  |  s.capitalize()

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Prefix/suffix kontrolü ve case dönüşümleri.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
'hello'.startswith('he')    # True
'hello'.endswith(('lo','ly'))  # True (tuple!)
'hello'.upper()              # 'HELLO'
'HELLO'.lower()              # 'hello'
'hello world'.title()        # 'Hello World'
'hello'.capitalize()         # 'Hello'

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: startswith/endswith tuple alır: s.startswith(('http','https')).

py01.title: dict.get() / setdefault()
py01.summary: get: güvenli erişim, fallback ile. setdefault: yoksa ekle ve değeri döner.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
d.get(key, default=None)
d.setdefault(key, default=None)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: get: güvenli erişim, fallback ile. setdefault: yoksa ekle ve değeri döner.

sections.heading: Örnek
Notlar
sections.text: get() KeyError önler. setdefault() gruplama için ideal (defaultdict daha iyi çoğunlukla).

py01.title: dict.keys() / values() / items()
py01.summary: Dict'i iterate etmek için view objeleri. Dict değişince dinamik güncellenir.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: d.keys()  |  d.values()  |  d.items()

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Dict'i iterate etmek için view objeleri. Dict değişince dinamik güncellenir.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
d = {'a':1, 'b':2, 'c':3}
for k in d:           # key iter
for k in d.keys():    # aynı
for v in d.values():  # değerler
for k,v in d.items(): # çiftler
'a' in d              # O(1) key check
list(d.values())      # [1,2,3]

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: View'lar dict'e bağlı. Loop sırasında dict değiştirme: list(d.items()) ile kopyala.

py01.title: Dict Comprehension / Merge
py01.summary: Compact dict oluşturma ve birleştirme.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
{k: v for k, v in iter if cond}
d1 | d2  (Python 3.9+)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Compact dict oluşturma ve birleştirme.

sections.heading: Örnek
Notlar
sections.text: Merge'de aynı key varsa sağ taraf kazanır. {**d1, **d2} dict() + update'den hızlı.

py01.title: collections.Counter
py01.summary: Dict subclass, sayma için. +/-/most_common() destekler. Eksik key 0 döner.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
from collections import Counter
Counter(iterable)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Dict subclass, sayma için. +/-/most_common() destekler. Eksik key 0 döner.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
from collections import Counter
c = Counter('abracadabra')
# Counter({'a':5,'b':2,'r':2,...})
c.most_common(3)    # [('a',5),('b',2),('r',2)]
c['z']              # 0  (KeyError yok!)
c1 + c2             # toplam
c1 - c2             # fark (sadece pozitifler)
Counter([1,1,2,3])[1]  # 2

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Anagram kontrolü: Counter(s1)==Counter(s2). c.total() Python 3.10+.

py01.title: collections.defaultdict
py01.summary: Eksik key'de factory() çağırarak default oluşturur. KeyError yok.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
from collections import defaultdict
defaultdict(factory)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Eksik key'de factory() çağırarak default oluşturur. KeyError yok.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
from collections import defaultdict
graph = defaultdict(list)
graph['a'].append('b')  # KeyError yok

count = defaultdict(int)
for w in words:
    count[w] += 1

groups = defaultdict(set)
for k, v in pairs:
    groups[k].add(v)

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: Factory: list, int, set, str, lambda: 0. Graph adjacency list için çok yaygın.

py01.title: Set İşlemleri
py01.summary: Sırasız unique elemanlar. O(1) ortalama lookup/insert/delete.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
s.add(x)  |  s.remove(x)  |  s.discard(x)
s1 | s2  &  -  ^

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: Sırasız unique elemanlar. O(1) ortalama lookup/insert/delete.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
s = {1, 2, 3}
s.add(4)         # {1,2,3,4}
s.remove(1)      # KeyError if missing
s.discard(99)    # hata yok
s1 | s2          # birleşim
s1 & s2          # kesişim
s1 - s2          # fark
s1 ^ s2          # simetrik fark
s1 <= s2         # subset mi?
set([1,1,2,3])   # {1,2,3} unique

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: discard() kullan, emin değilsen. set(lst) ile list'ten dup'ları sil. frozenset hashable.

py01.title: lambda / map() / filter()
py01.summary: lambda: isimsiz fonksiyon. map/filter lazy iterator döner.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text:
lambda args: expr
map(func, iter)  |  filter(func, iter)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: lambda: isimsiz fonksiyon. map/filter lazy iterator döner.

sections.heading: Örnek
sections.text:
sq = lambda x: x**2
sq(5)   # 25

list(map(lambda x: x**2, [1,2,3]))      # [1,4,9]
list(filter(lambda x: x>2, [1,2,3,4])) # [3,4]

# List comp çoğunlukla daha okunabilir:
[x**2 for x in [1,2,3]]
[x for x in [1,2,3,4] if x>2]

sections.heading: Notlar
sections.text: map(str, nums) gibi builtin'lerle kullanılabilir. Filter yerine list comp daha pythonic.

py01.title: *args / **kwargs
py01.summary: *args: pozisyonel argümanları tuple olarak toplar. **kwargs: keyword argümanları dict olarak.

sections.heading: Syntax
sections.text: def f(*args, **kwargs)

sections.heading: Açıklama
sections.text: *args: pozisyonel argümanları tuple olarak toplar. **kwargs: keyword argümanları dict olarak.

sections.heading: Örnek
Notlar
sections.text: * tek başına keyword-only zorunlu kılar. f(**d) ile dict'i unpack edebilirsin.

py01.title: any() / all()
