Connected-component labelling assigns a unique integer label to each maximal connected region using flood fill, enabling object counting, centroid computation, and region-of-interest extraction. This operation appears in every segmentation pipeline discussed in chapter ida05.

Morphological operations act on binary images via a structuring element $B$ — a small binary shape (disk, cross, square) that defines the neighbourhood of interaction. Dilation $A \oplus B = \{z : B_z \cap A \neq \emptyset\}$ grows regions outward; erosion $A \ominus B = \{z : B_z \subseteq A\}$ shrinks them. These two operations chain into opening ($A \circ B = (A \ominus B) \oplus B$, erosion then dilation) which removes small objects and smooths boundaries, and closing ($A \bullet B = (A \oplus B) \ominus B$, dilation then erosion) which fills small holes. Opening and closing are idempotent — applying them twice gives the same result as once — a useful algebraic property. These elementary operations remain in use throughout the course: morphological watersheds underpin classical segmentation (ida05), and the structuring element foreshadows the convolution kernel introduced systematically in ida02.
overviews.ida02:
If chapter ida01 establishes what an image is, chapter ida02 establishes the fundamental operations that transform images. Three mathematical frameworks are developed in parallel — spatial filtering via convolution, structural analysis via morphology, and frequency-domain analysis via the Fourier transform — and understanding their interrelationships is central to everything that follows in the course.

Discrete convolution is defined as $(I * h)[x,y] = \sum_{u,v} I[x-u, y-v]\, h[u,v]$, where $h$ is the filter kernel. The sign flip in the indices (correlation uses $+u,+v$; convolution uses $-u,-v$) matters when the kernel is not symmetric, but is irrelevant for symmetric kernels like Gaussians. The Gaussian filter $G(x,y;\sigma) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ is separable: $G(x,y;\sigma) = G(x;\sigma)\cdot G(y;\sigma)$. Separability enables a $M\times N$ convolution with a $k\times k$ kernel in $O(MNk)$ operations instead of $O(MNk^2)$ — a factor of $k$ reduction that matters enormously for large $k$. Gaussian smoothing at scale $\sigma$ removes structures finer than $\approx 2\sigma$ pixels, making it the canonical scale-space operator. The Laplacian of Gaussian (LoG) $\nabla^2 G$ combines smoothing with second-derivative sharpening: it detects blobs and edges as zero-crossings, and is equivalent to the difference of two Gaussians (DoG) at slightly different scales — a fact exploited by SIFT (chapter ida13).

Edge detection exemplifies the signal-processing perspective on images. The Sobel operator approximates the first spatial derivative: $\nabla I \approx (I * S_x, I * S_y)$ where $S_x$ and $S_y$ are $3\times3$ antisymmetric kernels. The gradient magnitude $|\nabla I| = \sqrt{(I*S_x)^2 + (I*S_y)^2}$ peaks at edges; the gradient direction $\theta = \arctan\!\left(\frac{I*S_y}{I*S_x}\right)$ indicates the edge orientation. The Canny detector (1986) adds two refinements: non-maximum suppression thins wide ridges to single-pixel edges by keeping only local maxima in the gradient direction; hysteresis thresholding uses two thresholds $T_{\text{low}}$ and $T_{\text{high}}$ to accept edge pixels — strong above $T_{\text{high}}$, weak only if connected to a strong pixel. This produces thin, connected edges robust to noise, and remains one of the most widely used preprocessing steps despite being over 35 years old.

The morphological operations introduced in ida01 receive their rigorous algebraic treatment here. Erosion and dilation are dual under complement: $\overline{A \oplus B} = \bar{A} \ominus B$, meaning dilation of the foreground equals erosion of the background. The hit-or-miss transform detects specific shapes by simultaneously eroding with a foreground pattern and eroding the complement with a background pattern, returning only pixels where both match simultaneously. Morphological gradient $A \oplus B - A \ominus B$ outlines object boundaries. These operations are exact and interpretable — important in scientific imaging contexts where a pixel-level explanation of every decision is required, as seen in the biological segmentation methods of chapter ida05.

The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) decomposes an image into complex sinusoids: $F(u,v) = \sum_{x=0}^{M-1}\sum_{y=0}^{N-1} I(x,y)\exp\!\left(-2\pi i\left(\frac{ux}{M}+\frac{vy}{N}\right)\right)$. The central coefficient $F(0,0)$ is the mean intensity (the DC component); coefficients at large $(u,v)$ capture fine spatial detail (high frequencies). The convolution theorem is the DFT's most powerful result: $\mathcal{F}\{I*h\} = \mathcal{F}\{I\} \cdot \mathcal{F}\{h\}$. Convolution in the spatial domain becomes pointwise multiplication in the frequency domain. For large kernels, it is faster to compute the DFT of both, multiply, and inverse-transform than to convolve directly. Frequency-domain analysis also makes the physics of filtering transparent: a low-pass filter (retain small $(u,v)$) blurs the image; a high-pass filter (retain large $(u,v)$) reveals edges; a notch filter removes specific spatial frequencies such as scan-line artefacts in microscopy. Truncating the frequency spectrum before inverse transform introduces Gibbs phenomenon (ringing near sharp edges) — the price paid for abrupt spectral cuts. The DFT perspective directly motivates why CNNs (ida03, ML ch09) learn to behave as adaptive band-pass filter banks.
overviews.ida03:
Chapters ida01 and ida02 develop classical, hand-engineered image analysis tools. Chapter ida03 introduces the data-driven paradigm that has superseded most of them: deep learning. Rather than deriving a specific algorithm for each image task, a neural network is trained end-to-end to learn the appropriate transformation directly from data. The chapter serves as a self-contained crash course, covering the same material as ML chapters ch07 (neural networks), ch08 (optimisation), and ch09 (CNNs) but with an emphasis on the image-specific design choices that make deep models work.

The multilayer perceptron (MLP) begins from the perceptron: a single linear threshold unit $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w^\top x + b)$. The XOR problem proved that no single hyperplane can classify all configurations, motivating hidden layers: with one hidden layer computing intermediate features, the input space is non-linearly remapped so that a final linear layer can separate any configuration. The formal MLP update rule is $a^{(l)} = g\!\left(W^{(l)} a^{(l-1)} + b^{(l)}\right)$, where $g$ is the elementwise activation function. Without $g$, the composition of linear layers collapses to a single linear transformation — depth becomes meaningless. The Universal Approximation Theorem guarantees that one hidden layer suffices to approximate any continuous function, but the exponential number of neurons required motivates the use of many layers with polynomial parameter counts. ReLU $\max(0,z)$ is the standard activation: its gradient is 1 for $z>0$ and 0 otherwise, preventing gradient attenuation for positive values and enabling training of very deep networks.

Backpropagation (Rumelhart et al., 1986) computes the gradient of the scalar loss with respect to every parameter in a single backward pass. Define error signals $\delta^{(l)} = \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial z^{(l)}$. At the output layer: $\delta^{(L)} = \frac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial a^{(L)}} \odot g'(z^{(L)})$. For each hidden layer: $\delta^{(l)} = \left(W^{(l+1)}\right)^\top \delta^{(l+1)} \odot g'(z^{(l)})$. Weight gradients: $\partial\mathcal{L}/\partial W^{(l)} = \delta^{(l)}(a^{(l-1)})^\top$. The key property is that computing all gradients costs $O(1)$ backward passes regardless of the number of parameters — not one pass per parameter — making training feasible for models with millions of weights. Vanishing gradients in deep networks (products of weight matrices and activation derivatives shrinking exponentially) are mitigated by ReLU activations, batch normalisation, and residual connections.

Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are the canonical architecture for image data, introduced in ML ch09 and applied throughout this course. Local receptive fields (each unit sees only a small patch), weight sharing (the same filter slides over the entire image), and pooling (local aggregation for translation invariance) together reduce parameters by orders of magnitude compared to fully-connected layers while encoding spatial inductive biases. A convolutional layer with $C_{\text{out}}$ filters of size $k\times k$ on input with $C_{\text{in}}$ channels has $k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}}$ parameters — independent of image size. Stacking convolutional layers, non-linearities, and pooling builds a hierarchy: early layers detect edges and textures; deep layers respond to semantic categories. This hierarchical feature learning is precisely what the subsequent detection and segmentation architectures (ida04, ida05) exploit.

The loss function and output activation must be jointly designed for the task. For regression (e.g., bounding box coordinates), a linear output with MSE loss $\mathcal{L} = \|y - \hat{y}\|^2$ is appropriate. For binary classification (foreground/background in segmentation), a sigmoid output $\hat{p} = \sigma(z)$ with binary cross-entropy $\mathcal{L} = -[t\log\hat{p} + (1-t)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ is standard. For $K$-class classification, softmax outputs $\hat{p}_k = \exp(z_k)/\sum_j\exp(z_j)$ with categorical cross-entropy. A critical design for imbalanced datasets — prevalent in detection where most locations are background — is the focal loss $\text{FL}(p_t) = -(1-p_t)^\gamma \log(p_t)$, which down-weights well-classified easy examples by factor $(1-p_t)^\gamma$ (typically $\gamma=2$), forcing the model to concentrate on the rare hard positives. Focal loss is the enabling component of RetinaNet and dense object detectors discussed in chapter ida04.
overviews.ida04:
Object detection asks not just 'what is in this image?' but 'where, and how many?'. This extension from classification to localisation constitutes one of the most impactful problems in computer vision, with direct applications in autonomous driving, medical image analysis, surveillance, and robotics. The chapter traces the evolution from classical sliding-window approaches to modern single-pass deep detectors, each transition motivated by a specific bottleneck in speed or accuracy.

Classical detection relied on template matching — scanning a reference patch over the image and finding locations of high correlation. This is conceptually equivalent to a single convolution (ida02 convolution theorem) but fails for objects with intra-class variability (different instances of 'car' look very different). Sliding-window detectors overcome this by training a strong binary classifier (an SVM with HOG features, or the Viola-Jones cascade for faces) and applying it exhaustively at multiple scales and aspect ratios. The Viola-Jones detector introduced two critical ideas: integral images for $O(1)$ feature computation at any scale, and cascaded classifiers that reject most windows at the first stage and only devote computation to promising candidates. Despite impressive real-time face detection performance, the hand-engineered feature pipeline fundamentally limits generalisation.

R-CNN (Girshick et al., 2014) replaced hand-crafted features with deep CNN representations. The pipeline is: (1) selective search generates $\approx2000$ region proposals per image based on colour and texture similarity; (2) each proposal is warped to $227\times227$ and forward-passed through a CNN independently; (3) the resulting features feed an SVM classifier and a bounding-box regressor. The accuracy was transformative; the speed was not — 47 seconds per image on a GPU, because $2000$ independent CNN passes are required. Fast R-CNN (2015) fixed the efficiency bottleneck by inverting the order of operations: run the CNN once on the whole image to get a shared feature map, then extract per-region features with RoI Pooling — a spatial max-pooling operation that maps any-sized region to a fixed $7\times7$ grid by dividing the region into a $7\times7$ grid and max-pooling within each cell. This reduced inference to $\approx2$ seconds per image, with the bottleneck now in selective search proposal generation.

Faster R-CNN (2015) eliminated selective search entirely by introducing the Region Proposal Network (RPN). The RPN slides a small network over the convolutional feature map and, at each spatial location, predicts objectness scores and bounding-box offsets for $k$ anchor boxes — predefined boxes of different scales and aspect ratios centred at that location. Anchors with IoU $>0.7$ with a ground-truth box are positive; IoU $<0.3$ are negative. The RPN and the detection head share the convolutional backbone, making the entire system trainable end-to-end. Inference requires $\approx0.2$ seconds per image — real-time for many applications. Faster R-CNN remains a standard two-stage baseline: high accuracy at the cost of two sequential networks.

YOLO (You Only Look Once, Redmon et al., 2016) reformulated detection as a single regression problem. The image is divided into an $S\times S$ grid (e.g., $7\times7$); each cell predicts $B$ bounding boxes parameterised as $(x,y,w,h,\text{confidence})$ and $C$ class probability vectors. Confidence is $P(\text{object}) \times \text{IoU}_{\text{pred,truth}}$. All predictions are made in a single forward pass, enabling 45 FPS detection on a GPU. The price is reduced accuracy on small objects and objects whose centres fall in the same cell. Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN, Lin et al., 2017) address multi-scale detection by adding a top-down pathway with lateral connections to the standard bottom-up CNN backbone. Each resolution level in the pyramid receives both fine-grained spatial information from the bottom-up path and rich semantic information from the top-down path, enabling accurate detection of objects across a wide range of scales. FPN is now a standard component in virtually all modern detectors. The focal loss introduced in ida03 was specifically designed for RetinaNet, a single-stage detector with FPN that achieves two-stage accuracy at single-stage speed — resolving the long-standing speed-accuracy trade-off.
overviews.ida05:
Detection (ida04) answers 'where is the object?' at bounding-box granularity. Segmentation goes further: it answers 'which pixel belongs to which object?' — a dense prediction problem requiring a label for every one of the $M\times N$ pixels in the image. This is both technically harder and scientifically more valuable: in biology, exact cell boundaries determine morphological measurements; in autonomous driving, knowing the precise road boundary is safety-critical.

Classical segmentation begins with global thresholding, the simplest possible region assignment. Otsu's method (1979) finds the threshold $T^*$ that minimises the weighted within-class variance of the two resulting regions, equivalent to maximising between-class variance. It derives $T^*$ analytically from the histogram — an $O(L)$ computation regardless of image size. Otsu assumes a bimodal intensity distribution; when this fails (uneven illumination, multiple objects), adaptive thresholding and more sophisticated methods are needed. The watershed algorithm treats the image gradient magnitude as a topographic surface: water poured into local minima rises and eventually meets water from adjacent basins, forming watershed lines — the segmentation boundaries. Watershed produces closed contours and handles complex topology but is sensitive to noise-induced local minima, requiring marker-controlled variants. Graph cuts formulate segmentation as a minimum-cut problem: pixels are graph nodes, edge weights encode colour similarity, and finding the minimum cut between source (foreground) and sink (background) terminals gives the globally optimal binary segmentation under the energy model — tractable via max-flow algorithms.

U-Net (Ronneberger et al., 2015) is the architecture that transformed biomedical image segmentation. The motivation was specific: biomedical datasets contain tens to hundreds of images rather than millions; the network must localise precisely (no image-level labels); and cell boundaries require fine-grained spatial resolution. U-Net's encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections addresses all three simultaneously. The encoder (contracting path) applies repeated $3\times3$ convolutions with $2\times2$ max-pooling, doubling channels at each level while halving spatial resolution — standard CNN feature extraction. The decoder (expansive path) upsamples via transposed convolutions, but critically, concatenates the corresponding encoder feature map via skip connections at each resolution level. These skip connections provide the decoder with precise spatial information that was lost during downsampling. The input is $572\times572$ but the output is $388\times388$ (valid convolutions, not padded) — the prediction covers only the central region to avoid boundary artefacts. Trained with aggressive data augmentation (elastic deformations, rotations, flips), U-Net achieves state-of-the-art results with as few as 30 training images.

Mask R-CNN (He et al., 2017) extends Faster R-CNN to instance segmentation: distinguishing between individual object instances, not just semantic categories. The extension adds a third head (alongside classification and bounding-box regression) that predicts a binary mask for each detected region. The critical technical contribution is RoI Align, which replaces the quantised RoI Pooling of Fast R-CNN with bilinear interpolation — avoiding the integer rounding errors that misalign features by up to 0.5 pixels. For $28\times28$ mask prediction, this misalignment is devastating; RoI Align eliminates it at negligible computational cost, substantially improving mask accuracy. Mask R-CNN achieves state-of-the-art instance segmentation and serves as the baseline for the biology-specific methods below.

Segmentation metrics are essential for model evaluation and comparison. Intersection over Union $\text{IoU} = |A\cap B|/|A\cup B|$ measures overlap between predicted and ground-truth regions; the Dice coefficient $\text{Dice} = 2|A\cap B|/(|A|+|B|)$ is mathematically equivalent to the F1-score and more sensitive near zero-overlap. Cellpose (Stringer et al., 2021) takes a fundamentally different approach for biological cell segmentation: instead of predicting masks directly, it predicts a gradient flow field pointing from every pixel toward the centre of its owning cell. Following these gradients by simulation groups pixels into cells — the method handles cells of arbitrary shape including elongated, branching, and densely packed morphologies that confound mask-based approaches. StarDist predicts for each pixel a set of radial distances to the cell boundary in $n$ directions, fitting a star-convex polygon — a shape that is the most natural representation for typical round/oval cell shapes in fluorescence microscopy. Both Cellpose and StarDist illustrate how domain knowledge of object geometry can be incorporated into neural network output parameterisations rather than treated as post-processing.
overviews.ida06:
Detection and segmentation (ida04, ida05) operate on individual frames. Many applications — autonomous driving, multi-object sports analysis, biological cell migration — require tracking objects continuously through time, associating detections across frames and maintaining persistent identities even through occlusions and re-entries. This chapter covers three complementary techniques: probabilistic state estimation with the Kalman filter, combinatorial assignment with the Hungarian algorithm, and dense motion estimation with optical flow.

The Kalman filter (Kalman, 1960) is the optimal linear estimator for a state-space model subject to Gaussian noise. The system model defines state dynamics: $\hat{x}_k = A\hat{x}_{k-1} + B u_k$ (prediction step), where $A$ is the transition matrix (e.g., constant-velocity motion: $A$ integrates velocity into position), $B u_k$ is optional control input, and prediction uncertainty grows as $P_k = A P_{k-1} A^\top + Q$ where $Q$ is the process noise covariance. The update step incorporates a new measurement $z_k$: compute the Kalman gain $K = P_k H^\top (H P_k H^\top + R)^{-1}$ where $H$ maps state to measurement space and $R$ is measurement noise covariance. The state and covariance are then corrected: $\hat{x}_k \leftarrow \hat{x}_k + K(z_k - H\hat{x}_k)$ and $P_k \leftarrow (I - KH)P_k$. The Kalman gain automatically balances trust in the prediction (shaped by $Q$) against trust in the measurement (shaped by $R$): high $R$ (noisy measurements) means trust the prediction more; high $Q$ (uncertain dynamics) means trust the measurement more. For non-linear dynamics (turning cars, swimming cells), the Extended Kalman Filter linearises around the current state; the Unscented Kalman Filter uses deterministic sigma points to propagate the full distribution non-linearly.

In multi-object tracking, detections from frame $t$ must be assigned to existing tracks from frame $t-1$. This is the assignment problem: given a cost matrix $C_{ij}$ (e.g., IoU between predicted track positions and detected bounding boxes), find the one-to-one assignment minimising total cost. The Hungarian algorithm (Kuhn, 1955; Munkres, 1957) solves this in $O(n^3)$ time, guaranteed globally optimal. The SORT algorithm (Simple Online and Realtime Tracking, 2016) combines Kalman prediction for bounding box state estimation with Hungarian assignment on IoU costs — achieving real-time multi-object tracking with minimal implementation complexity. DeepSORT extends SORT by incorporating appearance features from a deep CNN alongside IoU costs, enabling re-identification of objects returning after long occlusions where IoU-only cost fails. A track is terminated if unmatched for more than $T_{\text{lost}}$ frames and initialised for any unmatched detection.

Optical flow estimates the dense velocity field $\mathbf{v}(x,y) = (u,v)$ describing how each pixel moves between two consecutive frames. The brightness constancy assumption — $I(x,y,t) = I(x+u, y+v, t+1)$ — combined with first-order Taylor expansion gives the aperture problem equation: $I_x u + I_y v + I_t = 0$, a single equation in two unknowns, under-determined per pixel. Lucas-Kanade (1981) resolves this by assuming constant flow within a small window $\mathcal{W}$ and solving the resulting overdetermined system by least squares: $[u,v]^\top = (A^\top A)^{-1} A^\top b$ where rows of $A$ are $(I_x, I_y)$ for each window pixel. The matrix $A^\top A$ is the Harris structure tensor (also used for corner detection); the flow is reliable only where both eigenvalues are large — at corners, not along edges. Horn-Schunck (1981) instead imposes global smoothness: minimise $\int (I_x u + I_y v + I_t)^2 + \alpha(|\nabla u|^2+|\nabla v|^2)\,dx\,dy$, producing dense flow fields at the cost of blurring flow boundaries. FlowNet and subsequent deep architectures learn to predict optical flow end-to-end, dramatically improving accuracy on large-displacement and occluded regions. The flow field computed here feeds directly into temporal consistency constraints in video segmentation and action recognition pipelines.
overviews.ida07:
Chapters ida03–ida06 build on CNN-based architectures for every vision task. But CNNs have a structural limitation: their receptive field grows linearly with depth, requiring many layers to integrate information across large spatial distances. Self-attention, the mechanism developed for natural language in ML ch12, computes relationships between all pairs of positions simultaneously — $O(1)$ maximum path length regardless of image size. Chapter ida07 covers how this mechanism is adapted to vision and why it succeeds where CNNs struggle.

Self-attention (Vaswani et al., 2017; ML ch12) projects an input sequence into query, key, and value matrices: $Q = XW_Q$, $K = XW_K$, $V = XW_V$. The output is $\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^\top/\sqrt{d_k})V$. Each position's output is a weighted sum of all other positions' values, where the weights are dot-product similarities between queries and keys. Multi-head attention runs $h$ parallel attention computations in lower-dimensional subspaces, concatenates, and projects: $\text{MultiHead}(Q,K,V) = \text{Concat}(\text{head}_1,\ldots,\text{head}_h)W^O$. With $h=8$ heads and $d=512$, each head operates in a 64-dimensional space and specialises in different relationship types — syntactic, semantic, local, global. The critical property for vision is that the global attention map is computed from the very first layer: a pixel in the top-left corner can directly influence the representation of a pixel in the bottom-right corner without waiting for $\log_2(N)$ pooling layers.

The Vision Transformer (ViT, Dosovitskiy et al., 2020) applies the standard Transformer encoder directly to images by serialising them into patches. An image is divided into $16\times16$ non-overlapping patches, each flattened and linearly projected into a $d$-dimensional embedding. A learnable $[\text{CLS}]$ token is prepended; its output representation is used for classification. 1D learnable position embeddings are added to inform the model of patch positions, since self-attention is permutation-invariant. The sequence of $N+1$ tokens passes through $L$ standard Transformer encoder blocks (multi-head self-attention + layer norm + MLP + residual connections). The key finding: ViT matches or exceeds CNN accuracy, but only when pre-trained on very large datasets (ImageNet-21k with 14M images, or JFT-300M). With small datasets, CNNs' inductive biases (locality, translation equivariance) provide a significant advantage; ViT must learn these from data, requiring much more data to reach the same quality. Hybrid architectures prepend a CNN backbone to the Transformer, combining spatial efficiency with global attention.

DETR (DEtection TRansformer, Carion et al., 2020) applies the encoder-decoder Transformer architecture directly to object detection, eliminating the anchor boxes, RPN, and NMS post-processing that define the R-CNN family (ida04). A CNN backbone extracts a feature map; the Transformer encoder processes it with full self-attention; the decoder takes $N$ learnable object queries and attends to the encoder output via cross-attention. Each query produces one detection (class + bounding box), with unused queries predicting 'no object'. Training uses a bipartite matching loss: Hungarian algorithm matches the $N$ predictions to the ground-truth objects (padded to $N$), and only the matched pairs contribute to the loss. This set prediction formulation makes the detector permutation-invariant over predictions — no ordering is assumed — and eliminates redundant duplicate detections without NMS. DETR achieves Faster R-CNN accuracy on large objects but underperforms on small objects due to the global nature of encoder attention; this is addressed by Deformable DETR.

Swin Transformer (Liu et al., 2021) combines ViT's attention mechanism with the hierarchical feature pyramid of CNNs. The key innovation is windowed attention: rather than computing attention between all patch pairs (quadratic in image size), Swin restricts attention to non-overlapping local windows of $W\times W$ patches, giving $O(n)$ complexity in image size. Shifted window attention — alternating between regular and shifted window partitions across layers — enables cross-window connections without breaking the $O(n)$ complexity. A hierarchical architecture (patch merging at each stage) produces multi-resolution feature maps compatible with FPN-based detection and segmentation heads. Swin achieved state-of-the-art results on ImageNet classification, COCO object detection, and ADE20K semantic segmentation simultaneously, establishing it as the first ViT-based architecture that could serve as a universal vision backbone replacing CNNs.
overviews.ida08:
Chapter ida07 established Transformers as competitive with CNNs on standard vision benchmarks. Chapter ida08 pushes further, exploring architectures where the Transformer is not merely an add-on to a CNN backbone but the primary modelling component — for 3D medical image segmentation, promptable interactive segmentation, and self-supervised pre-training. These systems represent the current frontier of foundation models in visual analysis.

UNETR (Transformers for 3D Medical Image Segmentation, Hatamizadeh et al., 2022) adapts the ViT encoder for volumetric medical imaging. A 3D image volume (e.g., CT scan, $128\times128\times128$ voxels) is divided into $16\times16\times16$ patches, flattened, and linearly projected to form a sequence of tokens for a standard ViT encoder. The key architectural insight is that the Transformer encoder's intermediate representations, extracted at multiple depths, carry information at different semantic levels — similar to the encoder levels of U-Net. These intermediate tokens are reshaped from sequences back into volumetric feature maps and fed into a CNN decoder with skip connections, following the U-Net structure (ida05). The encoder captures long-range dependencies across the volume via global self-attention — impossible for CNNs within a single layer — while the CNN decoder provides precise boundary localisation at high resolution. UNETR substantially outperforms U-Net on complex 3D segmentation tasks, particularly for organs with large shape variability.

The Segment Anything Model (SAM, Kirillov et al., 2023) addresses a fundamentally different question: rather than training a specialised segmentor for one domain, can a single model segment any object in any image given a prompt? SAM introduces the concept of promptable segmentation: the model accepts a point, set of points, bounding box, or text as a specification of 'what to segment', and outputs a corresponding mask. The architecture has three components. The image encoder is a large ViT-H (huge ViT) that processes the input image once and produces a rich image embedding — this is the expensive step ($\sim90\%$ of compute). The prompt encoder converts geometric prompts (points, boxes) to embeddings using positional encodings, and text prompts via CLIP features. The mask decoder is lightweight: it uses cross-attention between prompt embeddings and image embeddings, followed by a simple MLP, to predict the mask in a few milliseconds. This asymmetry — expensive image encoder, cheap prompt decoder — enables interactive segmentation at interactive speeds. SAM was trained on SA-1B, a dataset of over one billion masks collected using a data engine that iteratively used SAM to assist human annotators. SAM achieves zero-shot generalisation to domains far from its training distribution, including microscopy, satellite imagery, and surgical video.

Masked Autoencoders (MAE, He et al., 2022) are a self-supervised pre-training method for ViTs. The core idea: mask 75% of image patches at random, pass only the visible patches through the ViT encoder, then pass the full set (visible + masked tokens) through a lightweight decoder to reconstruct the original pixel values of masked patches. This is asymmetric — the encoder only processes 25% of patches, making pre-training three times faster. The high masking ratio is critical: with low masking (e.g., 25%), simple interpolation between visible patches suffices; at 75%, the model must learn genuine semantic understanding to reconstruct coherent content. MAE pre-training produces ViT representations that fine-tune to state-of-the-art results on classification, detection, and segmentation with substantially fewer labeled examples than training from scratch.

DINO (self-DIstillation with NO labels, Caron et al., 2021) is a self-supervised method revealing a remarkable emergent property of Transformers: without any labels, the model learns to segment objects. DINO uses a student-teacher framework where both share the same ViT architecture. The student processes small local crops; the teacher (updated as an exponential moving average of the student) processes the full image. A cross-entropy loss between their output distributions (after a centering and sharpening operation to prevent collapse) drives the student to predict the teacher's global representation from local views. The striking result: the self-attention maps of the $[\text{CLS}]$ token in the final layer cleanly segment foreground objects from background, with no segmentation supervision — purely from the local-to-global consistency objective. This emergent segmentation property is not observed in CNN-based self-supervised methods, suggesting that Transformer attention specifically learns object-centric representations.
overviews.ida09:
All preceding chapters work with 2D images — a natural choice for cameras but not for 3D sensors. LiDAR sensors (in autonomous vehicles) and depth cameras (in robotics, AR/VR, medical imaging) produce point clouds: unordered sets of 3D coordinates $\{(x_i, y_i, z_i)\}_{i=1}^N$, each potentially with colour or intensity attributes. The challenge is fundamental: unlike images, point clouds have no regular grid structure, no fixed neighbourhood, and no canonical ordering. A neural network that receives the points in a different order must produce the same output — a property called permutation invariance that standard convolutions completely lack.

A point cloud from a LiDAR scan contains $\sim10^5$ points irregularly distributed in 3D space — dense near the sensor, sparse far away. Early approaches avoided the permutation problem by projecting point clouds onto 2D images (range images, bird's-eye-view) and applying standard CNNs. This discards 3D structure and introduces projection artefacts. Voxelisation discretises 3D space into a grid and applies 3D convolutions — but the cubic scaling of voxel count with resolution makes fine-grained representation prohibitively expensive. PointNet (Qi et al., 2017) is the first architecture to operate directly on raw point sets, achieving permutation invariance by design.

PointNet processes each point independently with a shared MLP (the same weights applied to every point, analogous to a $1\times1$ convolution in CNNs), then aggregates all point features via global max pooling: $g(\{x_1,\ldots,x_n\}) = \text{MAX}\{h(x_1),\ldots,h(x_n)\}$ where $h$ is the point-wise MLP and MAX is elementwise. Max pooling is symmetric under permutation and differentiable — backpropagation flows through it correctly. The critical points theorem formalises what the network actually learns: the output of global max pooling is determined by a small subset of the input points (those achieving the maximum in each feature dimension) called critical points. The network can be thought of as detecting a sparse set of characteristic point patterns; the remaining points are redundant. Before the MLP, a T-Net (Input Transform) predicts a $3\times3$ transformation matrix to align the input to a canonical orientation — making the network invariant to rigid transformations. A second Feature Transform aligns the intermediate feature space. The final architecture achieves strong performance on ModelNet40 classification and ShapeNet part segmentation with a remarkably simple design.

PointNet's critical limitation is the absence of local context: each point is processed independently before pooling. Two points from different objects could produce the same global descriptor if they each contribute the same maximum activations. Local geometric relationships — the curvature of a surface patch, the orientation of an edge — are not captured. PointNet++ (Qi et al., 2017) adds hierarchical local feature extraction. Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) selects a subset of representative centroids by iteratively choosing the point furthest from all already-selected points — ensuring coverage without gaps. Ball query groups all points within a radius $r$ around each centroid into a local neighbourhood. A small PointNet processes each neighbourhood independently, producing a feature vector for each centroid. This grouping-and-abstracting is repeated hierarchically: local neighbourhoods at one scale become individual points at the next. Multi-Scale Grouping (MSG) applies ball query at multiple radii simultaneously and concatenates the resulting features, handling the density variability inherent in LiDAR point clouds (close objects are dense; far objects are sparse). PointNet++ achieves substantially better performance than PointNet on complex shapes and connects directly to the advanced architectures explored in chapter ida12.
overviews.ida10:
Chapters ida03–ida09 focus on discriminative models that map inputs to labels or structures. Generative models take the opposite perspective: learn the data distribution $p(x)$ itself. This enables synthesising new images, learning compact latent representations without labels, translating between visual domains, and detecting anomalies as regions of low probability. Chapter ida10 covers three progressively more powerful generative frameworks: autoencoders, variational autoencoders, and generative adversarial networks.

An autoencoder consists of an encoder $E: \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Z}$ mapping inputs to a lower-dimensional latent code, and a decoder $D: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ mapping codes back to reconstructions. Training minimises the reconstruction loss $\mathcal{L} = \|x - D(E(x))\|^2$. The information bottleneck (the latent space is lower-dimensional than $x$) forces the encoder to compress meaningful structure; the decoder must recover the input from this compressed code. Autoencoders learn useful feature representations — the latent codes can serve as compact descriptors for retrieval or clustering — but the latent space has no probabilistic structure: there is no guarantee that a randomly sampled point in $\mathcal{Z}$ decodes to a realistic image. The latent manifold may have large gaps and discontinuities that produce nonsense when decoded.

The Variational Autoencoder (VAE, Kingma & Welling, 2013) adds probabilistic structure to the latent space. The encoder outputs parameters $\mu(x)$ and $\sigma^2(x)$ of a Gaussian posterior $q(z|x) = \mathcal{N}(z; \mu(x), \sigma^2(x) I)$; the decoder defines $p(x|z)$. Training maximises the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO): $\mathcal{L}_{\text{ELBO}} = \mathbb{E}_{q(z|x)}[\log p(x|z)] - \text{KL}(q(z|x)\|p(z))$. The first term rewards faithful reconstruction; the second penalises deviation of the posterior from the prior $p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0,I)$, regularising the latent space. The reparameterisation trick $z = \mu(x) + \varepsilon \cdot \sigma(x)$, $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$, makes the sampling step differentiable — the gradient flows through $\mu$ and $\sigma$ even though $z$ is stochastic. Forcing the posterior toward a standard Gaussian fills the latent space: interpolating between two codes $z_1$ and $z_2$ produces semantically smooth transitions through the data manifold. VAEs are the principled generative baseline from which diffusion models (ida11) are partly derived.

Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs, Goodfellow et al., 2014) train two networks in opposition. The generator $G: \mathcal{Z} \to \mathcal{X}$ maps random noise $z \sim p(z)$ to synthetic images. The discriminator $D: \mathcal{X} \to [0,1]$ predicts whether its input is real or generated. Training alternates between: updating $D$ to maximise $\mathbb{E}_{x\sim p_\text{data}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z\sim p_z}[\log(1-D(G(z)))]$; and updating $G$ to maximise $\mathbb{E}_{z}[\log D(G(z))]$ (making $D$ believe generated images are real). At Nash equilibrium, $G$ produces images indistinguishable from real data; $D$ outputs $0.5$ everywhere. In practice, GAN training is notoriously unstable: mode collapse (the generator produces a few high-quality samples but ignores most of the data distribution), vanishing gradients (when $D$ is too confident), and oscillation rather than convergence. DCGAN adds convolutional architectures, batch normalisation, and specific initialisation to stabilise training. Progressive GAN grows resolution from $4\times4$ to $1024\times1024$ incrementally. Spectral normalisation constrains the discriminator's Lipschitz constant.

Conditional generation enables controlled synthesis. pix2pix (Isola et al., 2017) learns paired image-to-image translation (e.g., semantic map $\to$ photo) by conditioning both $G$ and $D$ on the input image, with a combined L1 reconstruction loss and adversarial loss — L1 reduces blurriness; adversarial loss enforces realism. CycleGAN (Zhu et al., 2017) extends this to unpaired training: learn $G: X\to Y$ and $F: Y\to X$ simultaneously with cycle consistency losses $\mathcal{L}_{\text{cyc}} = \mathbb{E}[\|G(F(y))-y\|_1] + \mathbb{E}[\|F(G(x))-x\|_1]$, constraining the two generators to be approximate inverses. This enables domain translation without paired examples — style transfer between artistic styles, horse-to-zebra, summer-to-winter — and connects to the broader area of domain adaptation. The GAN framework's adversarial training idea is replaced by the more stable diffusion process in chapter ida11.
overviews.ida11:
GANs (ida10) generate high-quality images but are difficult to train and prone to mode collapse. Score-based generative models and diffusion models offer a principled probabilistic alternative: instead of learning to fool a discriminator, they learn to reverse a gradual noise corruption process. Diffusion models have become the dominant image synthesis paradigm, underlying Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 2, Imagen, and numerous scientific imaging applications.

The forward diffusion process adds Gaussian noise to data over $T$ steps, gradually destroying structure until the image is pure noise. Each step is defined by $q(x_t|x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(x_t; \sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,x_{t-1}, \beta_t I)$ where $\beta_t$ is a small variance schedule (linearly increasing from $\beta_1 = 10^{-4}$ to $\beta_T = 0.02$ over $T=1000$ steps). The key algebraic property is that $x_t$ can be sampled directly from $x_0$ without iterating through all $t$ steps: $x_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\,\varepsilon$ where $\bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^t(1-\beta_s)$ and $\varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$. At $t=T$, $\bar{\alpha}_T \approx 0$ and $x_T \approx \varepsilon$ — the image has been completely destroyed and replaced by Gaussian noise. This reparameterisation allows efficient training without sequential simulation.

The reverse process learns to denoise: given $x_t$, predict $x_{t-1}$. Because the true reverse conditional $q(x_{t-1}|x_t)$ depends on the entire data distribution, it is intractable. DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models, Ho et al., 2020) parameterises the reverse step as $p_\theta(x_{t-1}|x_t) = \mathcal{N}(x_{t-1}; \mu_\theta(x_t, t), \Sigma_\theta(x_t, t))$ and trains a neural network $\varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t)$ to predict the noise $\varepsilon$ that was added in the forward step. The simplified training objective is $\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{t, x_0, \varepsilon}\left[\|\varepsilon - \varepsilon_\theta(\sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\varepsilon,\, t)\|^2\right]$ — just mean squared error between the true noise and the predicted noise. The architecture is a U-Net (ida05) with time-step conditioning (sinusoidal embeddings of $t$ injected at each layer via FiLM — Feature-wise Linear Modulation). At inference, $T=1000$ sequential denoising steps are required — substantially slower than GANs.

DDIM (Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models, Song et al., 2020) reformulates the reverse process as a non-Markovian deterministic trajectory using the same trained $\varepsilon_\theta$: $$x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_{t-1}}\underbrace{\frac{x_t - \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\varepsilon_\theta}{\sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}}}_{\hat{x}_0\text{ estimate}} + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_{t-1}}\varepsilon_\theta.$$ Since the trajectory is deterministic, large steps can be skipped: 50 DDIM steps produce quality comparable to 1000 DDPM steps, a $20\times$ speedup. Classifier-free guidance (Ho & Salimans, 2022) enables conditional generation without a separate classifier. A single network $\varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c)$ is trained with conditioning $c$ (class label, text embedding) randomly dropped out with probability 10–20%, so the model learns both conditional and unconditional denoising. At inference, the guided prediction is $\hat\varepsilon = \varepsilon_\theta(x_t,t,\varnothing) + w\cdot(\varepsilon_\theta(x_t,t,c) - \varepsilon_\theta(x_t,t,\varnothing))$, where $w > 1$ is the guidance scale. High $w$ produces images that strongly match the condition but lack diversity; $w=1$ recovers the conditional model. Stable Diffusion (Rombach et al., 2022) applies the diffusion process not in pixel space but in the latent space of a pre-trained VAE (ida10), reducing computational cost by $8\times$ in each spatial dimension while preserving perceptual quality. Text conditioning is provided by CLIP text embeddings via cross-attention in the U-Net. The connection to score matching is exact: $\varepsilon_\theta \approx -\sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\nabla_{x_t}\log q(x_t)$, so DDPM noise prediction is equivalent to estimating the score (gradient of log-density).
overviews.ida12:
Chapter ida09 established PointNet and PointNet++ as the foundational architectures for point cloud processing. While PointNet++ adds local context through hierarchical grouping, it still relies on fixed neighbourhoods defined in Euclidean space. Chapter ida12 explores three architectures that address remaining limitations: robust density-adaptive convolution (FCPN), dynamic feature-space graph convolution (DGCNN), and shape generation through surface folding (FoldingNet).

Fully Convolutional Point Networks (FCPN, Rethage et al., 2018) tackle a practical limitation of both PointNet and PointNet++: sensitivity to non-uniform point density. LiDAR scans inherently produce denser point clouds for nearby surfaces and sparser ones for distant surfaces. Standard ball-query grouping (PointNet++) captures many points near the sensor and few far away, producing inconsistent local feature aggregation. FCPN replaces ball query with a sliding cylinder kernel: a vertical cylinder of fixed radius and height is placed at each centroid, and all points within it are aggregated. Cylinder grouping is invariant to the vertical distribution of points (important for outdoor 3D scenes where height is qualitatively different from lateral extent). Local convolution within the cylinder uses shared MLP features, similar to PointNet, but with the cylinder geometry providing a consistent spatial context. The fully convolutional design (no fixed-size global pooling) produces per-point or per-voxel predictions naturally, enabling dense semantic segmentation of large-scale outdoor scenes.

DGCNN (Dynamic Graph CNN, Wang et al., 2019) reformulates local feature aggregation as an operation on a dynamically constructed graph. For each point $x_i$, the $k$ nearest neighbours in the current feature space are found, and edge features are computed: $e_{ij} = h_\Theta(x_i,\, x_j - x_i)$ where $h_\Theta$ is a shared MLP applied to the concatenation of the central point feature and the relative neighbour feature (difference $x_j - x_i$ encodes local geometry). The EdgeConv operation aggregates over the $k$ edges: $x_i' = \max_{j\in\mathcal{N}(i)} e_{ij}$. The critical word is 'dynamic': the $k$-NN graph is recomputed in the updated feature space after each EdgeConv layer. In the first layer, neighbours are the $k$ nearest points in 3D Euclidean space (geometric proximity). In deeper layers, neighbours are found in learned feature space — two points on opposite ends of an elongated object may be far in Euclidean space but close in feature space (similar geometry), and DGCNN captures this non-local semantic relationship. This graph-rewiring across layers is the key distinction from PointNet++ (fixed Euclidean grouping). DGCNN achieves state-of-the-art results on both classification and part segmentation, with the dynamic graph naturally adapting to the task.

FoldingNet (Yang et al., 2018) addresses point cloud generation and reconstruction — the inverse problem of analysis. Given a point cloud, encode it into a compact codeword; given a codeword, decode a point cloud that approximates the original shape. The encoder is a PointNet-like architecture producing a fixed-length codeword $\mathbf{c} \in \mathbb{R}^{512}$. The decoder is the novel contribution: it takes a fixed 2D grid of points (parameterising a flat surface) and deforms it into a 3D surface approximating the input shape through two sequential folding operations. Each folding step concatenates the 2D grid coordinates (or the intermediate folded coordinates) with the codeword $\mathbf{c}$ and applies a shared MLP to produce 3D offsets. Two folding operations are used because one folding can only represent locally simple (nearly flat) deformations; the second folding takes the result of the first (which has folded the sheet into a rough 3D shape) and refines fine-grained surface details. Reconstruction quality is measured by Chamfer distance: $d_{\text{CD}}(S_1,S_2) = \sum_{x\in S_1}\min_{y\in S_2}\|x-y\|^2 + \sum_{y\in S_2}\min_{x\in S_1}\|y-x\|^2$, which is symmetric, differentiable, and does not require point correspondence. FoldingNet's codewords serve as shape embeddings for retrieval and interpolation: interpolating linearly between two codewords and decoding produces a morphologically smooth transition between two 3D shapes, analogous to the VAE latent space behaviour discussed in ida10.
overviews.ida13:
The penultimate chapter examines three techniques for making image analysis systems interpretable and for understanding the representations they learn. As deep networks replace classical algorithms in scientific computing, medical imaging, and safety-critical applications, the ability to explain why a model produced a particular output, and to visualise what it has learned, is increasingly important — both for debugging and for building justified trust.

Bag of Visual Words (BoVW) is not an interpretability method but the dominant classical image classification framework before deep learning, presented here as the historical baseline that contextualises the shift to learned representations. The pipeline has three stages. First, local feature descriptors (typically SIFT — Scale-Invariant Feature Transform) are extracted from keypoints detected at multiple scales. SIFT descriptors are 128-dimensional gradient orientation histograms, invariant to scale, rotation, and illumination changes. Second, $k$-means clustering on a large collection of descriptors defines a visual vocabulary (codebook) of $K$ visual words (typically $K \in [500, 5000]$). Third, each image is encoded as a histogram of visual word occurrences: each descriptor is assigned to its nearest codebook entry, and the image representation is the count vector $h \in \mathbb{R}^K$. A SVM classifier trained on these histograms achieves competitive accuracy on benchmark datasets. The limitation is fundamental: the codebook is fixed and task-agnostic; the histogram discards all spatial relationships between visual words; and SIFT's hand-engineered gradient orientation basis is not optimal for any specific task. End-to-end CNNs overcome all three limitations simultaneously — the connection between BoVW and CNN feature learning (both count aggregate occurrences of local patterns) is more than metaphorical: the first convolutional layer learns filters analogous to SIFT gradient templates, and spatial pyramid pooling extends BoVW with coarse spatial binning.

GradCAM (Gradient-weighted Class Activation Mapping, Selvaraju et al., 2017) answers the question: which spatial regions of the input image did the network attend to when predicting class $c$? The method is architecture-agnostic to the early layers — only the final convolutional feature maps $A^k \in \mathbb{R}^{H\times W}$ are required. Channel-wise importance weights are computed as $\alpha^c_k = \frac{1}{Z}\sum_{i,j}\frac{\partial y^c}{\partial A^k_{ij}}$ — the spatially-averaged gradient of the class score $y^c$ with respect to the $k$-th activation map. The saliency map is $L^c_{\text{GradCAM}} = \text{ReLU}\!\left(\sum_k \alpha^c_k A^k\right)$, upsampled to input resolution and overlaid on the image as a heat map. The ReLU keeps only features positively associated with class $c$ (negative values indicate features of the complementary class). GradCAM is used for debugging — does the model look at the dog or the background when predicting 'dog'? — and for discovering bias. Guided Backpropagation combines GradCAM with the constraint that gradients are only backpropagated through ReLU units where both the gradient and the activation are positive, producing sharper, pixel-level attribution maps that highlight fine-grained texture evidence rather than coarse spatial regions. GradCAM++, Score-CAM, and Eigen-CAM are subsequent refinements addressing specific failure modes.

Dimensionality reduction visualises high-dimensional representations in 2D or 3D. PCA projects data onto the directions of maximum variance via eigendecomposition of the covariance matrix — a linear, global method that preserves large pairwise distances but collapses non-linear cluster structure. t-SNE (Stochastic Neighbour Embedding with $t$-distribution, van der Maaten & Hinton, 2008) is the standard for visualising deep representations. High-dimensional affinities are defined as $p_{j|i} = \exp(-\|x_i-x_j\|^2/2\sigma_i^2)/\sum_{k\neq i}\exp(-\|x_i-x_k\|^2/2\sigma_i^2)$, with $\sigma_i$ chosen by binary search to achieve a target perplexity (typically 30–50), and symmetrised: $p_{ij} = (p_{j|i}+p_{i|j})/2N$. Low-dimensional affinities use a Student-$t$ distribution with one degree of freedom: $q_{ij} = (1+\|y_i-y_j\|^2)^{-1}/\sum_{k\neq l}(1+\|y_k-y_l\|^2)^{-1}$. The heavy tail of the $t$-distribution compensates for the crowding problem: in high dimensions, points can be far apart in many directions simultaneously, but in 2D there is not enough space. The $t$-distribution places well-separated clusters further apart than Gaussian low-dimensional affinities would, producing the characteristic tight clusters with visible inter-cluster gaps. Training minimises $\text{KL}(P\|Q)$ by gradient descent. t-SNE preserves local neighbourhood structure faithfully but does not preserve global distances — two clusters appearing close in the 2D plot may not be genuinely similar. UMAP (McInnes et al., 2018) is a graph-based alternative derived from Riemannian geometry and topological data analysis; it preserves both local and some global structure and runs faster than t-SNE, making it the method of choice for very large datasets (millions of points) such as single-cell RNA sequencing.

summary.title: ML Dersi — Baştan Sona Motivasyon Zinciri
summary.subtitle: Her adım bir öncekinin en kritik limitasyonunu çözüyor

chain.title: Temel Hedef: Posterior Bulmak
chain.what: ML'in özü şu: elimde bir veri noktası var, hangi sınıfa ait? Bayes teoremi ideal çerçeveyi veriyor — prior (sınıf olasılıkları) ve likelihood (bu sınıftan bu verinin gelme olasılığı) bilersen posterior'ı, yani 'bu veri X sınıfından' olasılığını hesaplayabilirsin. Teorik olarak optimal karar bu.
problems: Likelihood'u bilmiyoruz — gerçek dünyada p(x|C) dağılımı elimizde yok
Veriden tahmin etmek gerekiyor
chain.next: Dağılımı veriden tahmin eden yöntemlere ihtiyaç var

chain.title: Dağılımı Veriden Tahmin Et
chain.what: Verinin belli bir dağılımdan geldiğini varsay (genelde Gaussian), sonra o dağılımın parametrelerini (μ, Σ) veriden Maximum Likelihood Estimation ile tahmin et. Az veriyle hızlı çalışır.
problems: MLE, az veri durumunda variance'ı küçük tahmin eder (underfit), fazla veride overfit eder
Gerçek veri Gaussian olmayabilir — tek Gaussian karmaşık şekilleri ifade edemez
Dağılımın şeklini önceden bilmek gerekiyor — varsayım yanlışsa model tamamen çöküyor

chain.title: Varsayımsız Yoğunluk Tahmini
chain.what: Dağılım varsayımı yapmadan doğrudan veriden yoğunluk tahmin et. Histogram basit ama bin genişliği kritik. Kernel Density her veri noktasına kernel koyar. k-NN ise en yakın k komşuya bakarak yoğunluğu tahmin eder.
problems: Curse of dimensionality — boyut arttıkça ihtiyaç duyulan veri miktarı üstel büyüyor
Tüm training seti bellekte saklanmak zorunda
k ve bandwidth gibi parametrelere aşırı hassas
Histogram yüksek boyuta ölçeklenmiyor
chain.next: Daha esnek ama hesaplanabilir bir yoğunluk modeli lazım

chain.title: Birden Fazla Gaussian'ı Birleştir
chain.what: Tek Gaussian yetmiyorsa birden fazlasını topla — karmaşık dağılımları ifade edebilir. Ama parametreleri tahmin etmek için hem ortalama hem responsibility bilinmesi lazım, ikisi de birbirine bağımlı, kapalı form çözüm yok. EM: önce sabit responsibility ile parametreleri güncelle, sonra sabit parametrelerle responsibility'leri güncelle, tekrarla.
problems: Initialization'a çok hassas — k-means ile initialize edilmesi gerekiyor
K seçimi zor: kaç Gaussian kullanmalı?
Outlier'lara hassas
Hesaplama pahalı ve yavaş
Hâlâ generative yaklaşım — p(x)'i modelliyoruz ama aslında sadece karar sınırı istiyoruz
chain.next: Dağılımı modellemeyi bırak, direkt karar sınırını bul

chain.title: Direkt Sınır Bul: Discriminative Yaklaşım
chain.what: Generative'in temel sorunu: p(x|C) tahmin etmek zor ve gereksiz karmaşık. Biz sadece karar sınırını bulmak istiyoruz. Direkt ağırlık vektörü öğren: y(x) = wᵀx + w₀. Least Squares ile kapalı form çözüm var, hızlı.
problems: Least Squares outlier'lara aşırı hassas — uzaktaki tek bir nokta tüm sınırı kaydırır
Çıktı 0-1 arasında kalmıyor, olasılık yorumu zor
Hâlâ linear sınır — verinin linearly separable olduğu varsayılıyor
chain.next: Outlier dayanıklılığı ve olasılık yorumu için sigmoid gerekiyor

chain.title: Çıktıyı Sıkıştır, Olasılık Yorumu Kazan
chain.what: Least squares çıktısını sigmoid fonksiyonuyla [0,1]'e sıkıştır — artık olasılık yorumu var, outlier etkisi azaldı. Cross-entropy loss ile optimize edilir, gradient descent gerekiyor. Sigmoid neden? σ(z) = 1/(1+e⁻ᶻ) — herhangi bir gerçel sayıyı (0,1) arasına sıkıştırır, her noktada diferansiyellenebilir, p = 0.5 eşiği doğal karar sınırı verir. Ama z'nin mutlak değeri büyüdükçe gradient sıfıra yaklaşır (doygunlaşır) — bu vanishing gradient sorununa yol açar, bu yüzden derin ağların hidden layer'larında sigmoid artık kullanılmıyor, ReLU tercih ediliyor.
problems: Hâlâ linear sınır — linearly separable olmayan problemler (XOR gibi) çözülemiyor
Feature engineering hâlâ gerekiyor
chain.next: Non-linear sınırlar için veriyi farklı uzaya taşımak gerekiyor

chain.title: Non-Linear Uzaya Taşı
chain.what: Veriyi önce non-linear bir fonksiyonla dönüştür φ(x), sonra bu yeni uzayda linear model kur. XOR gibi problemler artık çözülebilir hale geliyor.
problems: Hangi basis function'ları seçeceksin? Elle seçim gerekiyor — domain knowledge lazım
Yüksek boyutlu feature uzayı hesaplama patlamasına yol açıyor
Genel bir yöntem değil
chain.next: Elle seçim olmadan en iyi margin'i bul, ve kernel ile boyut sorununu çöz

chain.title: En Geniş Margin'i Bul
chain.method: Support Vector Machines — Hard & Soft Margin + Kernel
chain.what: Sadece sınırı bulmak değil, iki sınıf arasındaki boşluğu maksimize et (max-margin). Sadece support vector'lar modeli belirliyor. Soft margin ile outlier toleransı eklendi. Kernel trick ile veriyi yüksek boyuta taşıyıp orada linear SVM uygulamak orijinal uzayda non-linear sınıra denk geliyor — explicit dönüşüm yapmadan.
problems: Kernel seçimi hâlâ manuel ve domain-specific
Training O(n²-n³) — büyük veri setlerinde çok yavaş
Image/text gibi yüksek boyutlu ham veri için hangi kernel kullanılacak belli değil
Feature representation hâlâ dışarıdan veriliyor — model kendi feature'larını öğrenemiyor
chain.next: Feature representation'ı da modelin öğrenmesi gerekiyor

chain.title: Zayıfları Birleştir
chain.what: Tek güçlü model yerine çok sayıda zayıf classifier birleştir. Her round'da bir öncekinin yanlış yaptıklarına daha fazla ağırlık ver, yeni bir classifier eğit. Exponential loss minimize ettiği gösterilebilir. Overfitting'e şaşırtıcı derecede dayanıklı.
problems: Noise ve outlier'lara hassas — hatalı etiketli noktalar çok yüksek ağırlık alır
Sequential eğitim — paralelize edilemiyor
Hâlâ elle hazırlanmış feature'lara bağımlı
chain.next: Feature öğrenimi için tamamen farklı bir yaklaşım: Neural Networks

chain.title: Kayıp Fonksiyonlarını Karşılaştır
chain.method: Loss Functions — MSE · Cross-Entropy · Hinge · Exponential
chain.what: Her öğrenme algoritması bir kayıp fonksiyonu minimize eder — bu seçim modelin hassasiyetini, gradient davranışını ve outlier toleransını doğrudan belirler. MSE (Karesel Hata): L = (1/n)Σ(y−ŷ)² — büyük hataları karesel cezalandırır, outlier'a aşırı hassas. Regresyon için doğal ama sınıflandırma için kötü: çıktı sınırsız ve gradient davranışı ideal değil. Cross-Entropy (Log Loss): Sınıflandırma standardı. Binary: L = −[y·log(p) + (1−y)·log(1−p)]. Modelin tahmini ne kadar 'şaşırdı' sorusuna cevap verir. Sigmoid ile kombinasyonu güzel gradient üretir — MSE+sigmoid vanishing gradient'a uğrar, cross-entropy+sigmoid vermez, bu yüzden logistic regression ve neural network'ler cross-entropy kullanır. Multi-class için Softmax + Categorical Cross-Entropy: L = −Σyₖ·log(pₖ). Softmax: softmax(z)ₖ = eᶻᵏ/Σeᶻʲ — tüm sınıf skorlarını toplamı 1 olan olasılık dağılımına dönüştürür; biri artınca diğerleri azalır (competitive normalization), sigmoid'in çok sınıflı genellemesi değil. Hinge Loss: L = Σmax(0, 1−y·f(x)). SVM'nin kaybı — doğru sınıflandırılmış ve margin içindeyse sıfır kayıp, sadece support vector'lar gradient üretiyor. Exponential Loss: L = Σexp(−y·f(x)). AdaBoost'un minimize ettiği kayıp. Büyük hataları çok agresif cezalandırır — AdaBoost'un gürültülü etiketlere hassasiyetinin sebebi bu.
problems: Regresyon → MSE, Sınıflandırma → Cross-Entropy, Margin-based → Hinge: doğru eşleştirme önemli
MSE+sigmoid sınıflandırmada kötü gradient verir — cross-entropy+sigmoid çok daha sağlıklı
Exponential loss outlier'a en hassas olan — AdaBoost'un gürültüye zayıflığının matematiksel sebebi
Softmax büyük logit farklarında nümerik instability — pratikte log-softmax ile stabilize edilir
chain.next: Feature'ları da modelin öğrenmesi gerekiyor → Neural Networks

chain.title: Feature'ları da Öğren
chain.what: Basis function'ları elle seçmek yerine, verinin bize öğretmesine izin ver. MLP her katmanda non-linear dönüşümler uygulayarak hiyerarşik feature'lar öğreniyor. Teorik olarak universal approximator. Backpropagation ile gradient chain rule üzerinden tüm ağırlıklar güncelleniyor. Aktivasyon fonksiyonu kritik: sigmoid doygunlaşıp vanishing gradient'a yol açıyor, tanh biraz daha iyi ama aynı sorun var. ReLU (max(0,z)) bunu çözüyor — z > 0 için gradient sabit 1, hesaplama ucuz, derin ağlarda standart. Çıktı katmanında ise problem tipine göre: ikili sınıflandırma için sigmoid + binary cross-entropy, çok sınıflı için softmax + categorical cross-entropy, regresyon için linear + MSE.
problems: Sığ ağlar yeterince güçlü değil — derin yapmak gerekiyor
Vanishing gradient: gradyanlar geri gittikçe katmanlar boyu küçülüyor, ilk katmanlar öğrenemiyor
Overfit, yavaş eğitim, hyperparameter hassasiyeti
Image'daki spatial structure görmezden geliniyor — 224×224 image için milyonlarca parametre
chain.next: Image için spatial yapıyı kullanan mimari gerekiyor

chain.title: Spatial Yapıyı Kullan
chain.what: Weight sharing (aynı filtre tüm image üzerinde kaydır) ve local connectivity ile parametre sayısı dramatik düşüyor. İlk katmanlar edge, sonrakiler texture, derin katmanlar obje parçaları öğreniyor. AlexNet (2012) ImageNet'te fark yaratarak CNN çağını başlattı.
problems: Daha derin = daha iyi? Hayır — degradation problemi: çok derin ağlarda training error bile artıyor
Bu overfitting değil, optimization problemi
50+ katmandan sonra eğitim çöküyor
chain.next: Derinliği artırmanın önündeki engeli kaldırmak gerekiyor

chain.title: Derinlik Engelini Aş
chain.what: Eğer derin katmanlar öğrenemiyorsa, en azından identity mapping yapsın — skip connection ile girdi direkt çıktıya ekleniyor. Ağ artık 'ne öğreneceğini' değil 'ne ekleyeceğini' öğreniyor (residual). Gradient highway etkisi sayesinde 100+ katlı ağlar başarıyla eğitildi.
problems: Metin, ses, zaman serisi gibi sequential veri için hâlâ çözüm yok
CNN sabit boyutlu input varsayıyor, değişken uzunluklu sequence'leri işleyemiyor
chain.next: Sequential veri için özel mimari gerekiyor

chain.title: Metni Temsil Et
chain.what: One-hot encoding çok boyutlu ve anlamsız — 'kral' ile 'kraliçe' arasında hiçbir ilişki yok. Word2Vec kelimeleri dense vektörlere map eder, anlamsal ilişkileri yakalar. 'kral − erkek + kadın ≈ kraliçe' gibi vector arithmetic çalışıyor.
problems: Her kelimenin tek ve sabit bir vektörü var — context'e göre anlam değişmiyor
'bank' nehir kenarı mı banka mı? Her iki durumda da aynı vektör
Sıralı yapı ve uzun bağımlılıklar hâlâ çözülmedi
chain.next: Sıralı yapıyı ve context'i işleyen model gerekiyor

chain.title: Sırayı Öğren
chain.what: Önceki adımların bilgisi hidden state üzerinden taşınıyor. Metin, ses, zaman serisi gibi sıralı veriler için doğal yapı. Her adımda hem girdi hem önceki state kullanılıyor.
problems: Vanishing/exploding gradient — uzun sequence'lerde çok önceki bilgi yok oluyor
'Dün gittiğim markette gördüğüm ___' — cümle uzadıkça RNN başını unutuyor
Long-range dependency'leri öğrenemiyor
chain.next: Uzun bağımlılıkları tutabilen hafıza mekanizması gerekiyor

chain.title: Uzun Hafızayı Koru
chain.what: Kapı mekanizması (forget, input, output gate) ile hangi bilginin tutulacağını, hangisinin unutulacağını ağ öğreniyor. Cell state sayesinde gradient'ler uzun süre taşınabiliyor. Seq2Seq mimarisi makine çevirisi için encoder-decoder yapısı. Attention: decoder her adımda encoder'ın tüm state'lerine bakıp hangisine odaklanacağını öğreniyor.
problems: Hâlâ sequential — paralelize edilemiyor, uzun sequence'ler çok yavaş
Eğitim süresi büyük modellerde pratik değil
Çok uzak bağımlılıklar hâlâ sınırlı
chain.next: Sequential yapıyı tamamen ortadan kaldır, her şeyi paralel yap

chain.title: Her Şeyi Paralel Yap
chain.what: RNN'i tamamen at, sadece attention kullan. Her token tüm diğer tokenlarla paralel olarak ilişki kuruyor. Self-attention: aynı sequence içindeki tokenlar arası ilişki. Multi-head attention birden fazla farklı ilişki tipini aynı anda öğreniyor. Positional encoding ile sıra bilgisi ekleniyor. BERT ve GPT bu mimarinin üzerine inşa edildi.
chain.next: Günümüz AI'ının temeli — LLM, Vision Transformer, Diffusion model hepsinin backbone'u

bigpicture: Bayes · optimal ama dağılımı bilmek lazım
↓  Parametric PDE · dağılımı öğren ama varsayımlı
↓  MoG + EM · esnek ama pahalı ve yavaş
↓  Linear Discriminants · direkt sınır bul ama linear
↓  Logistic Regression · sigmoid ile olasılık yorumu ama hâlâ linear
↓  Basis Functions · non-linear ama elle seçim
↓  SVM + Kernel · optimal margin + kernel trick ama feature öğrenemiyor
↓  AdaBoost · ensemble gücü ama hâlâ dışarıdan feature
↓  Loss Functions · MSE · Cross-Entropy · Hinge · Exponential — kayıp seçimi davranışı belirler
↓  MLP · feature'ları öğren, ReLU+Softmax, ama spatial kör, vanishing gradient
↓  CNN · spatial yapı + weight sharing ama çok derin = degradation
↓  ResNet · residual ile derinlik engeli aşıldı
↓  RNN · sequence öğren ama uzun bağımlılık yok
↓  LSTM · uzun hafıza ama sequential → yavaş
↓  Transformer · tam paralel, uzun bağımlılık, ölçeklenebilir ✓
